title: A Literature Review of Robust Variance Estimation for Bayesian Meta-Analysis with Dependent Effect Sizes
text:
Preface
This book positions and motivates Cholesky Calibration Correction (CCC), a Bayesian calibration method for meta-analysis with dependent effect sizes. It is a claim-anchored literature review: it assembles the case for CCC by tracing one object through three literatures, and it does not re-derive the method. The formal results live in companion manuscripts (in preparation), cited here only as motivation. CCC is an original coinage for the meta-analytic operationalization of the Cholesky scale-and-shape projection mechanism of Williams and Savitsky; the mechanism is theirs, while the name and the meta-analytic instantiation are original contributions (G-06). The intended reader is a methodologist who already works with random-effects and multilevel meta-analysis and recognizes robust variance estimation (RVE) as the frequentist standard for dependent effects.
The funnel, in one paragraph. The single object the book tracks is the marginal RVE/CHE sandwich covariance — the covariance target that frequentist robust variance estimation fixes for dependent-effect meta-analysis. The argument descends in four beats. The frequentist RVE lineage, from Hedges, Tipton and Johnson (2010) through Pustejovsky and Chen (2024), made the robust cluster-sandwich the structural standard and so owns that target (P1). The Bayesian hierarchical and multilevel meta-analysis tradition has the modeling apparatus to host a posterior but has not adopted that covariance as a calibration target, so it lacks the join (P2). The calibrated-Bayes philosophy of Little (2012) and Rubin (1984), which judges a Bayesian model against frequentist operating characteristics, preserving the inference center while realigning covariance, supplies the license that permits importing the target (P3). CCC delivers the join, matching the named marginal RVE/CHE sandwich covariance exactly in finite samples at the unit-weight boundary, with predictor-specific diagnostics motivated for downstream work (P4).
How to read it (HTML-first). The book renders as a navigable HTML Quarto Book, built to be moved through rather than read front-to-back. Seven chapters carry the funnel, with a structured literature-database appendix beneath them:

Ch. 1 — Introduction & the inferential mismatch: the three literatures and the join this review proposes.
Ch. 2 — The frequentist RVE lineage owns it (P1).
Ch. 3 — Bayesian meta-analysis & the missing analogue (P2).
Ch. 4 — The calibrated-Bayes lens (P3).
Ch. 5 — Why Bayesian RVE specifically (P4).
Ch. 6 — The gap, the contribution & claim-type discipline.
Ch. 7 — Boundaries & open questions.
Appendix — the literature database and pillar-by-theme coverage map.

Use the sidebar to move between chapters, the in-page table of contents to move within one, the search box to find a method or author, and the clickable DOIs to reach every cited source. Cross-references between chapters are live links, so a reader can follow one claim across the funnel without losing place.
Standing disciplines. Three conventions run through every chapter and serve as a reading key. First, name the covariance target every time: wherever the text touches a sandwich or a calibration, it names the specific object, the marginal RVE/CHE sandwich covariance, so that frequentist and Bayesian idioms never blur. Second, claim-typing: each load-bearing claim is tagged inline with its identifier and type (THEOREM, CONDITIONAL, EMPIRICAL, HEURISTIC, GAP-ASSERTION, or POSITIONING), which fixes the evidence the claim is entitled to and lets a reader see at a glance what stands behind a sentence. Third, covariance-matching is not coverage (G-09): matching is an exact, finite-sample, algebraic property of the calibrated posterior’s covariance relative to the named target, whereas coverage is a separate operating characteristic, asymptotic in the number of studies and shown by simulation; the two are held in separate sentences and never conflated. One further book-level discipline applies: the gap the book documents is a documented systematic absence (G-23), phrased as an unoccupied conjunction rather than a proof that no such method could exist. The book motivates CCC; it does not re-prove it.
The funnel that follows tracks the object through these literatures in four beats: RVE owns it, Bayesian meta-analysis lacks it, calibrated-Bayes permits it, and CCC delivers it, with diagnostics.

title: 1  Introduction & the inferential mismatch
text:
1.1 1.1 The three-literatures mismatch
Defining a Bayesian robust variance estimator for dependent-effect meta-analysis is the work of three literatures, not one. This chapter names the three literatures, the join this review proposes between them, and the roadmap of the funnel the rest of the book descends. The object the funnel tracks — the marginal RVE/CHE sandwich covariance — is introduced here and never leaves: every chapter hands the same target to the next.
The problem this book addresses is an inferential mismatch. Defining such an estimator is the work of three literatures rather than one, each supplying one necessary but individually insufficient component (CL-P0-01, HEURISTIC; G-24). Naming the mismatch first, rather than any one field, matters because the contribution is a join across fields rather than an improvement within one, and the join is invisible until all three components are named together.
The first component is robust cluster-sandwich estimation, originating with Hedges et al. (2010). This lineage fixes the target: it points a cluster-robust sandwich at the marginal covariance of a dependent effect-size vector through a working model (aggregated or correlated-effects) that may misspecify the within-study correlation, and it secures consistency in the number of studies without requiring the true within-study correlation to be known. The marginal RVE/CHE sandwich covariance is the object this literature owns, and the object the other two literatures will be asked to host. What this literature does not supply is a posterior: it furnishes a covariance target and an estimator for it, not a Bayesian inferential machine.
The second component is the Bayesian hierarchical and multilevel meta-analysis apparatus. This tradition supplies the modeling machinery: weakly informative heterogeneity priors, observation-level random effects, multivariate and three-level structures, and the posterior as the primary inferential object. Its strength is expressiveness: it can represent within- and between-study dependence hierarchically and preserve observation-level design matrices. What it does not, on its own, supply is a named covariance target. Because the Bayesian analyst keeps the posterior as the primary object, a frequentist sandwich covariance is not a standard reporting quantity in this literature; the modeling apparatus is present, but the target the first literature owns is not among its objectives.
The third component is the calibrated-Bayes philosophy of Little (2012) and Rubin (1984). Here a Bayesian model is judged against frequentist operating characteristics, and the governing move is to preserve the inference center while realigning the covariance toward a frequentist operating-characteristic target. These two works establish the principle as standing prior art. This literature supplies the license (the warrant for importing a frequentist covariance target into Bayesian inference), but it is a general principle rather than a meta-analytic method, and it points at no particular target and no particular application. Crediting the principle to Little and Rubin is a binding discipline (G-24): this review invents neither calibrated Bayes nor the idea of preserving the center while realigning covariance; only the application of that principle to the meta-analytic sandwich target is novel here.
Each component is necessary, and none suffices alone. The first owns a covariance target but offers no posterior; the second owns a posterior but names no covariance target; the third owns the license that would authorize importing the one into the other, yet it is prior art that has not been aimed at the target. The mismatch is therefore structural rather than a matter of any field’s deficiency: three mature literatures supply three parts of a single inferential object, and that object is well-defined only when the parts are assembled at the point where they meet, the covariance.

title: 1  Introduction & the inferential mismatch
section: 1.2 1.2 The join this review proposes
text:
1.2 1.2 The join this review proposes
The program’s move is to perform that assembly explicitly, carrying the marginal RVE/CHE sandwich covariance as the object held across all three literatures. The frequentist RVE lineage, from Hedges et al. (2010) through Pustejovsky & Chen (2024), made the robust cluster-sandwich the structural standard for dependent-effect meta-analysis, fixing the marginal RVE/CHE sandwich covariance as the target any analogue would have to match; calibrated-Bayes theory (Little, 2012; Rubin, 1984) supplies the intellectual license to import that same sandwich covariance into Bayesian inference as an explicit calibration target (CL-P0-02, POSITIONING; G-23, G-24, G-27). The two streams are real and independently established: the sandwich-as-standard clause is anchored at its origin in Hedges, Tipton and Johnson (2010) and capped by Pustejovsky and Chen (2024), while the license clause rests on Little and Rubin in the survey and official-statistics context where those authors worked. Stated as a movement of one object, the proposal is to take the covariance target the frequentist lineage already owns and, under the calibrated-Bayes license, adopt it as the object a Bayesian posterior is calibrated to match, preserving the posterior mean while realigning its covariance toward that target.
This conjunction has not been occupied, and the claim that it has not is made here conditionally and defended downstream. Across a broad survey of Bayesian meta-analysis, these three literatures have not been joined at the relevant point: no published meta-analysis has pointed the calibrated-Bayes principle at the marginal RVE/CHE sandwich covariance as an explicit calibration target. The wording governs the whole book. The conjunction is unoccupied, not unattainable: each ingredient already exists in print (posterior recoloring toward some sandwich, robust sandwich estimation, and the calibrated-Bayes principle have each been used somewhere), and this review claims no priority over any single one (G-27). Both qualifiers are retained, the RVE/CHE sandwich target and the Little/Rubin license, so that near-neighbors which recolor toward a different sandwich under a different license are not miscounted as occupants. The assertion rests on a documented systematic search, its strength equals the breadth of that search, and it is never escalated to a proof that no such method could exist (G-23). The verbatim negative-existence statement is therefore deferred: it is asserted once, with its full search breadth, where the Bayesian gap is established (Chapter 3), and restated as positioning where the contribution is assembled (Chapter 6). Here the claim is only that the conjunction is open and that this review proposes to occupy it.
The absence admits a non-pejorative reading. It reflects no deficiency in the Bayesian apparatus, only that a posterior calibrated to a marginal RVE/CHE sandwich covariance has not been a standard object in this literature. What is missing is not Bayesian capacity but the application of an established license to an established target. The principle that licenses the occupation remains Little’s and Rubin’s; the survey-to-Bayes mechanism this review later adapts remains Williams and Savitsky’s; what is new is the application: pointing an established principle, through an adapted mechanism, at a target the frequentist literature already owns (G-24).

title: 1  Introduction & the inferential mismatch
section: 1.3 1.3 Roadmap of the funnel
text:
1.3 1.3 Roadmap of the funnel
The rest of the book is one descent, tracking the marginal RVE/CHE sandwich covariance through the three literatures in four beats.

P1 — RVE owns it (Ch. 2). The frequentist RVE lineage fixed the marginal sandwich covariance as the estimand, disciplined the small-sample regime with named corrections, and shipped the canonical software. This chapter establishes the target the rest of the funnel must match.
P2 — Bayesian meta-analysis lacks it (Ch. 3). The Bayesian tradition arrives at that target with ample modeling capacity and no analogue. This chapter documents the gap (an absence of application, not of Bayesian capacity) and hosts the verbatim gap statement.
P3 — calibrated-Bayes permits it (Ch. 4). The calibrated-Bayes principle supplies the missing license, the Williams–Savitsky survey-to-Bayes Cholesky mechanism supplies the operation, and this review names the result CCC. This chapter shows the join is permitted and how it is built.
P4 — CCC delivers it, with diagnostics (Ch. 5). A specifically Bayesian RVE earns its place through capabilities the frequentist sandwich does not readily afford (flexible heterogeneity priors, observation-level structure, and per-coefficient design-effect-ratio diagnostics), with covariance-matching and coverage held strictly separate.

The two unoccupied conjunctions then close affirmatively in Ch. 6, where the gap, this review’s matching results, and the matching-versus-coverage distinction are assembled into the stated contribution and the claim-type discipline is set out as a reading key; Ch. 7 bounds the contribution, framing its scope as preconditions rather than apologies. The first beat begins now: the next chapter develops only the first conjunct, namely what that owned frequentist standard is and what covariance it targets.




Hedges, L. V., Tipton, E., & Johnson, M. C. (2010). Robust variance estimation in meta-regression with dependent effect size estimates. Research Synthesis Methods. https://doi.org/10.1002/jrsm.5


Little, R. J. (2012). Calibrated bayes, an alternative inferential paradigm for official statistics. Journal of Official Statistics.


Pustejovsky, J. E., & Chen, M. (2024). Equivalencies between ad hoc strategies and multivariate models for meta-analysis of dependent effect sizes. Journal of Educational and Behavioral Statistics. https://doi.org/10.3102/10769986241232524


Rubin, D. B. (1984). Bayesianly justifiable and relevant frequency calculations for the applied statistician. The Annals of Statistics. https://doi.org/10.1214/aos/1176346785

title: 2  The frequentist RVE lineage owns it
text:
2.1 2.1 The established field-default
This chapter establishes the first leg of the funnel: the marginal RVE/CHE sandwich covariance that any Bayesian analogue must match is a settled, owned frequentist standard. It is owned not by a single paper but by a lineage: one apparatus that matured, link by link, from Hedges, Tipton and Johnson’s origin through Tipton’s small-sample discipline and Pustejovsky and Tipton’s working-model consolidation to Pustejovsky and Chen’s integrative capstone. The chronology carries the argument. Each link extends rather than displaces the one before it, so that what arrives at the end is the completion of a single standard rather than a competing estimator. The object that lineage owns is a specific covariance estimand, the marginal RVE/CHE sandwich covariance of the effect-size vector, and the conditions under which each of its properties holds are stated with care below, because it is that estimand, with those conditions, that any Bayesian analogue must measure itself against. The seven sections trace that maturation: what the lineage made the field-default, what its sandwich targets, why the target is robust, how it disciplined the small-sample regime, why CR2 is a comparator and not the target, which working model survives where the other breaks, and the degrees-of-freedom tuning and canonical software that instantiate the whole apparatus. This chapter renders the standard; it does not re-prove it.
Robust variance estimation is the established frequentist field-default for meta-analysis with dependent effect sizes, built from the cluster-sandwich and refined across the lineage from Hedges, Tipton and Johnson (2010) through Tipton (2015; 2015), Pustejovsky and Tipton (2022), to Pustejovsky and Chen (2024); its field-default status is documented both in the methodological lineage and in the practitioner-tutorial literature (Tanner-Smith et al., 2016) (CL-P1-01, POSITIONING). This is a framing claim about the structure of the frequentist literature, not a program theorem: the status is carried as POSITIONING, and the origin paper’s own theorem is held at the field level, never re-elevated.
Hedges et al. (2010) is the origin: it establishes the cluster-sandwich as the frequentist apparatus for dependent-effect meta-regression, furnishing both a correlated/observation-level working model and the cluster-level residual-cross-product sandwich that estimates the marginal covariance of the effect-size vector. Tipton then disciplined the small-sample regime that the origin result left asymptotic (Tipton, 2015) and extended the standard to multiple-contrast tests (Tipton & Pustejovsky, 2015), each move extending rather than overturning Hedges, Tipton and Johnson. Pustejovsky and Tipton (2022) consolidated the working-model choices — correlated-effects, hierarchical-effects, and their combination — into a single deployable framework, and Pustejovsky and Chen (2024) integrated the apparatus into one treatment of aggregated and correlated-effects representations, supplying the analytic equivalence and design-class results this chapter leans on. The status is documented from two directions at once. The methodological lineage names and extends the chain: the more recent multivariate and cross-domain entries (Chen & Pustejovsky, 2025; Welz et al., 2023; Yang et al., 2024) inherit the same sandwich without overturning it, and Pustejovsky (2025) names the same lineage as the “well-developed and readily available” robustness strand for dependent effects. From the other direction, the practitioner tutorials (Tanner-Smith et al., 2016; Tanner-Smith & Tipton, 2014) and applied syntheses (Cameron & Miller, 2015; Gnambs & Schroeders, 2023) document cross-disciplinary adoption across education, psychology, health, and the microeconometric clustering literature from which the cluster-sandwich also descends, and the canonical software lineage (Fernández-Castilla et al., 2026; Joshi et al., 2022; Williams et al., 2025) carries it into routine use.
The field-default claim is made honestly, with its adoption qualifier intact and stated as a precondition rather than a concession. Tipton, Pustejovsky and Ahmadi (2019) record that RVE was endorsed in principle among the consensus-recommended modeling alternatives for dependent effects, yet appeared in only about five percent of syntheses as of 2016. “Field-default” therefore denotes methodological consensus and emerging adoption (the standard the field recognizes as correct for dependent effects, the one the methods literature converged on and the tutorials teach) rather than universal practitioner uptake. The qualifier sharpens the lineage claim rather than weakening it. The review measures a Bayesian analogue against the estimand this lineage fixed, an object whose authority rests on the lineage’s structural coherence, not on a head-count of applied syntheses.

title: 2  The frequentist RVE lineage owns it
section: 2.2 2.2 What the sandwich targets
text:
2.2 2.2 What the sandwich targets
The lineage having fixed which apparatus is standard, the next link fixes what that apparatus targets. The RVE sandwich targets the marginal covariance of the effect-size vector through a working model — aggregated or correlated-effects — that may misspecify the within-study correlation, and is asymptotically consistent in m, the number of studies, without requiring the true within-study correlation to be known (CL-P1-02, CONDITIONAL; G-03, G-15). Pustejovsky and Chen (2024) state the property directly: the sandwich does not rely on the assumed dependence structure being correctly specified, and it requires a large number of independent clusters. The covariance the sandwich estimates is the marginal covariance — the object that survives integrating over whatever within-study correlation the working model posits — and naming it as marginal is a standing discipline of this book. Two preconditions on that consistency are load-bearing and must be kept distinct.
The consistency runs in m (studies), not in k (effect sizes). The estimand is a marginal covariance estimated from cluster-level residual cross-products, and the origin result is explicitly contingent on regularity conditions that bite only as the number of independent studies grows (Hedges et al., 2010; Pustejovsky & Tipton, 2022). A synthesis with twelve studies reporting ten effects each has k = 120 rows in its data frame but m = 12 independent clusters; it is the twelve that the asymptotics count. The cross-domain analogues confirm the same direction under the clusters↔︎studies mapping: econometric clustering theory makes consistency contingent on the number of clusters diverging, not the number of observations (Cameron & Miller, 2015), and the survey design-based sandwich is consistent in the survey central-limit sense, in the number of sampled units (Binder, 1983), with clusters and strata playing the role of studies, never effect sizes. Eliding m into k would misstate the asymptotic regime, and it is precisely the regime where the small-m discipline of §2.4 becomes binding (G-15).
What Pustejovsky and Chen establish about the two working-model likelihoods is a proportionality, not an identity. Their Theorem 2 shows the aggregated and correlated-effects likelihoods are proportional — equal up to a parameter-free constant κ, and not identical (G-03). The distinction is load-bearing for the entire book: proportionality up to a constant suffices for the maximizing arguments and the sandwich target to agree, which is what licenses moving between the two representations, but it is strictly weaker than a likelihood identity. Exact identity is a separate, stronger statement reserved downstream for this review’s own unit-weight-boundary result; it must never be read back into the field’s proportionality theorem. The cross-domain analogues confirm the weaker, correct reading: econometric clustering (Cameron & Miller, 2015) and survey design-based estimation (Binder, 1983) each affirm consistency in the number of clusters, and neither asserts a likelihood identity. What the sandwich targets, then, is a single marginal covariance, reached through either of two proportionally related working models, consistent as studies accumulate.

title: 2  The frequentist RVE lineage owns it
section: 2.3 2.3 Why the sandwich is robust
text:
2.3 2.3 Why the sandwich is robust
Why the marginal sandwich is robust is the link that explains why the lineage’s target is worth calibrating to at all. The sandwich estimator remains consistent under essentially arbitrary misspecification of the within-study correlation structure (which is almost never known in meta-analytic practice), provided the working model’s marginal moments are calibrated to the data-generating process (Cameron & Miller, 2015; Hong et al., 2018; Pustejovsky & Tipton, 2022) (CL-P1-03, CONDITIONAL; G-25). This is the sandwich’s central virtue: its validity does not turn on getting the dependence structure right, only on the marginal moments being approximately correct. Hong et al. (2018) states the rationale plainly: the estimator is asymptotically correct even when the working likelihood is incorrect, and the consolidating lineage (Pustejovsky & Tipton, 2022) carries it as an asymptotic-in-m property of the field. The robustness is asymptotic and conditional on calibrated marginal moments; it is not unconditional, and the prose states the condition with the claim.
The deeper rationale is supplied by the estimating-equation and Godambe-information machinery, of which the design-based sandwich of Binder (1983) is the survey-side origin. That substrate gives the asymptotic justification for why covariance recalibration under working-model misspecification is principled rather than ad hoc: when an estimator solves an estimating equation whose score is correct in expectation, its sampling covariance takes the Godambe sandwich form regardless of whether the full working likelihood is correct, given correctly specified low-order margins. This is the precise sense in which the substrate motivates the need for a correction — it establishes that a marginal covariance target is well-defined and that realigning toward it is licensed by estimating-equation theory — without itself supplying a finite-sample correction (G-25). The literature carries it as motivation, never as proof that a finite-sample correction already exists (Joshi et al., 2022). This misspecification-robustness, asymptotic, conditional, and principled, is what makes the marginal sandwich covariance an attractive calibration target for a Bayesian analogue: it is the covariance an estimating-equation argument says inference should report under misspecification. It is cited here as why-recalibration-is-needed framing, the hinge the calibrated-Bayes pillar (P3) later inherits, not as evidence the correction has already been built.
crumbs: 2  The frequentist RVE lineage owns it

title: 2  The frequentist RVE lineage owns it
section: 2.4 2.4 The small-m discipline
text:
2.4 2.4 The small-m discipline
The robustness of §2.3 is asymptotic, and the asymptotics bite slowly, which is why the lineage’s most consequential refinement was the small-sample discipline. In small-m meta-analysis, especially m below eight, RVE inflates Type I error unless a named small-sample correction is applied: HTZ, AHZ, CWB, or CR2 (Bell–McCaffrey; Imbens & Kolesár (2016)); uncorrected RVE does not control Type I error when studies are few (CL-P1-04, EMPIRICAL; G-15). This is the chapter’s spine guard, and it is stated qualitatively by discipline: uncorrected small-m RVE over-rejects, and level control recovers only after a named correction. No bare coverage percentage is asserted, and no sentence claims small-m adequacy without naming the specific adjustment that delivers it.
Tipton (2015) is the primary named source. Tipton showed by simulation that uncorrected robust inference over-rejects badly in small-m meta-regression and that level control recovers only via the bias-reduced residual corrections together with Satterthwaite degrees of freedom — the named HTZ-type adjustment. Each subsequent link in the lineage named its own correction, again extending the discipline rather than replacing it. Tipton & Pustejovsky (2015) extended it to multiple-contrast tests with the AHZ correction, showing uncorrected multiple-contrast RVE liberal even at large m. Joshi et al. (2022) carried the cluster wild bootstrap (CWB) as a named small-m alternative, directly stating that small-m RVE inflates Type I error and is controlled only through a named correction. Yang et al. (2024) confirmed that the bare cluster-robust estimator is downwardly biased and fails Type I control unless the number of independent studies is large or a named correction is applied. Beneath this meta-analytic lineage sits its methodological origin: Bell & McCaffrey (2002) derived the bias-reduced linearization later named CR2 and showed by simulation that uncorrected small-sample linearization inflates Type I error, and Pustejovsky & Tipton (2018) implemented CR2 with Satterthwaite/AHT degrees of freedom and documented the over-rejection of the uncorrected Wald test.
The same discipline is corroborated, in its own idiom, by adjacent settings, and the boundary between the four endorsed corrections and these analogues is held explicit. The econometric clustering literature from which the cluster-sandwich descends finds that few-cluster Wald tests over-reject unless a named correction — the cluster jackknife CV3 with a t(G−1) reference (MacKinnon et al., 2023, 2025) or the Bell–McCaffrey degrees-of-freedom adjustment (Cameron & Miller, 2015) — is applied; small-k scalar-summary intervals undercover unless an adjusted reference distribution is used (Zejnullahi & Hedges, 2024); the general-meta-analytic precursor literature on liberal small-sample tests names its own remedy, the Hartung q-variance estimator with a t reference (Hartung & Knapp, 2001); and the multilevel literature warns that ignoring the within-study sampling covariance underestimates standard errors and inflates Type I error (Van Den Noortgate et al., 2015). These cross-domain corrections are corroboration that the small-sample failure and its named remedies are general; the CV3 jackknife and the t(G−1) reference in particular are kept clearly labeled as econometric analogues, not as one of the four meta-analytic corrections the review endorses under G-15. What a working meta-analyst must take from this section is a rule, not a number: in the small-m regime, RVE adequacy is a claim about RVE-plus-a-named-correction, never about bare RVE. An adequacy claim that does not name its correction is not a claim the test supports.
crumbs: 2  The frequentist RVE lineage owns it

title: 2  The frequentist RVE lineage owns it
section: 2.5 2.5 CR2 is a comparator, not the target
text:
2.5 2.5 CR2 is a comparator, not the target
Among the named corrections, CR2 warrants particular care, because it is the comparator most easily mistaken for a universal fix. The CR2 correction (Bell–McCaffrey; Imbens & Kolesár (2016)) improves coverage on balanced designs but degrades under severe imbalance or skewed regressors, and its compound-symmetric and constant-subgroup-size conditions are not always met (CL-P1-05, CONDITIONAL; G-04, G-14). Even at its canonical development the correction is not universal: Imbens & Kolesár (2016) show that Bell–McCaffrey is nearly inert on balanced designs, helps most under skew or imbalance, yet itself undercovers under skewed errors at one extreme and is conservative at the other. The underlying bias-reduced linearization is exactly unbiased only under independent and identically distributed errors (Bell & McCaffrey, 2002), so the equality it promises is a design-specific property, not a guarantee that holds across designs.
The conditions under which CR2 achieves its equivalence are pinned by the lineage’s capstone. Pustejovsky and Chen (2024, Theorem 3) establish that the CR2-type equivalence holds only under compound-symmetric within-subgroup blocks and constant subgroup size — conditions the paper characterizes as more restrictive and artificial, and which fail in Pustejovsky and Chen’s own worked examples (G-04). Where those conditions break, so does the equality. The degradation is documented across designs: coverage falls materially below nominal in small-k multivariate meta-regression (Welz et al., 2023), and most starkly in crossed phylogenetic designs, where CR2 is inapplicable and cluster-robust inference falls below nominal regardless (Williams et al., 2025). Because CR2’s accuracy is contingent on design balance, it cannot serve as ground truth; the prose never describes it as universal, as covering all designs, or as eliminating Type I error (G-14). CR2 is therefore a design-specific tuning and an external comparator — useful where its design conditions hold, degraded where they do not — never a universal fix and never the calibration target. This is the distinction the chapter carries forward: the object a Bayesian analogue must match is the marginal RVE/CHE sandwich covariance itself, not CR2 treated as ground truth. CR2 is one design-aware way the frequentist lineage approximates that covariance in finite samples; it is not the covariance.
crumbs: 2  The frequentist RVE lineage owns it

title: 2  The frequentist RVE lineage owns it
section: 2.6 2.6 The design-class distinction
text:
2.6 2.6 The design-class distinction
The lineage also fixes which working model survives when the data structure is most demanding, a distinction this review later inherits as the warrant for observation-level Bayesian structure. Dependent effects are handled by either the aggregated (one-row-per-study) or the correlated-effects (observation-level) working model; when moderators vary within study, the aggregated representation ceases to exist, whereas the correlated-effects model remains well-defined with the sandwich applied at the observation level (Pustejovsky & Chen, 2024) (CL-P1-06, CONDITIONAL; G-05). The mechanism is plain: the aggregated one-row-per-study representation requires study-level predictors, so when a moderator takes different values across the effect sizes within the same study, there is no single study-level row that can represent it, and the aggregated representation ceases to correspond to any model. The correlated-effects model, working at the level of the individual effect size, retains the full within-study design matrix and simply keeps the rows.
The direction of this result is locked (G-05): it is aggregation that breaks, and the correlated-effects model that survives, never the reverse. Inverting it (claiming the correlated-effects model fails) would misstate the entire design-class result and quietly undercut the observation-level structure this review carries into its Bayesian analogue. The “ceases to exist” result itself is sourced uniquely to Pustejovsky and Chen (2024), the capstone of the lineage; the earlier links and the parallel literatures furnish the two representations and corroborate the direction without asserting the analytic result themselves. Hedges et al. (2010) supplies both the correlated/observation-level working model and the synthetic aggregated alternative but does not state the direction, which is Pustejovsky and Chen’s contribution; and the multilevel route reaches the same design-class distinction from the hierarchical side, with Van Den Noortgate et al. (2015) showing that aggregating effect sizes loses information and can make moderator estimation impossible while the observation-level model survives, the same direction, never inverted. For a practitioner with within-study moderator variation, the operational consequence is direct: the observation-level correlated-effects model is not merely the more flexible choice but the only well-defined one. This survival of observation-level structure is the analytic warrant for the Bayesian observation-level model this review carries into P4: a Bayesian analogue that operates at the observation level is calibrating to a target that remains well-defined precisely where the aggregated alternative cannot.
crumbs: 2  The frequentist RVE lineage owns it

title: 2  The frequentist RVE lineage owns it
section: 2.7 2.7 Satterthwaite df tuning and canonical tooling
text:
2.7 2.7 Satterthwaite df tuning and canonical tooling
Operating the sandwich in finite samples requires a reference distribution, and the lineage’s routine device for setting it is the last refinement to trace, together with the software that instantiates the whole apparatus. The Satterthwaite degrees-of-freedom approximation is routinely applied in frequentist RVE meta-regression to set the effective degrees of freedom for tests and confidence intervals; it is asymptotically justified but biased under severe imbalance or small m, becoming unreliable once the effective degrees of freedom fall below roughly four (Pustejovsky & Tipton, 2018; Tipton, 2015) (CL-P1-07, CONDITIONAL; G-15). Here too the chronology is one of refinement: the origin paper (Hedges et al., 2010) supplied only the crude m − p correction; the coefficient-by-coefficient Satterthwaite approximation, and the finding that the effective df are covariate-dependent and often far below m − 1, are established by Tipton (2015) and Pustejovsky & Tipton (2018), with Joshi et al. (2022) corroborating both the routine use and the small-m below-nominal behavior. The practical rule that falls out, familiar from any clubSandwich or robumeta warning, is to distrust a test whose effective df drop below about four, because the Satterthwaite reference is no longer reliable there. This df-below-four fragility is exactly the kind of finite-sample imperfection in the field-default tooling whose own limits under small m and imbalance are part of the standing small-sample discipline (G-15), and it ties directly back to §2.4.
That entire apparatus — sandwich, named corrections, Satterthwaite df — is, finally, instantiated in canonical software, which is what carries the standard from the methods literature into a practitioner’s analysis (CL-P1-08, POSITIONING). The clubSandwich package (Pustejovsky and Tipton) and robumeta (Fisher and Tipton) are the canonical implementations of frequentist RVE with small-sample corrections (CR2, HTZ/AHZ, and Satterthwaite degrees of freedom) and the field-standard tooling for dependent-effect meta-analysis (Joshi et al., 2022; Pustejovsky & Tipton, 2018; Tanner-Smith et al., 2016; Welz et al., 2023). Pustejovsky and Tipton (2018) are the methods source that introduces and implements CR2 with AHT degrees of freedom in clubSandwich; Joshi and colleagues (2022) and Tanner-Smith and Tipton (2016) name and use both clubSandwich and robumeta as the standard implementations actually deployed; Welz et al. (2023) names clubSandwich as the canonical software for cluster-robust estimation with small-sample corrections; and the field-tooling record (Fernández-Castilla et al., 2026; Tipton et al., 2019), with Pustejovsky and Tipton (2022), situates them in the broader practitioner ecosystem. The lineage thus closes the loop a practitioner cares about: a fixed estimand, a disciplined small-sample regime with named corrections, a monitored reference distribution, and shipped software that does all three from a single deployable toolchain.
Taken together, CL-P1-01 through CL-P1-08 establish a complete, instantiated, software-backed standard, and they fix a single object: a marginal cluster-sandwich, consistent in m under working-model misspecification, robust under calibrated marginal moments, proportional-not-identical across its two working-model representations, requiring a named small-sample correction at low m, with CR2 as a design-specific comparator rather than the target, the aggregated representation that breaks where the correlated-effects model survives, the Satterthwaite tuning with its df-below-four fragility, and clubSandwich and robumeta as canonical tooling. This is the owned frequentist standard — one apparatus matured into a field-default through the lineage from Hedges, Tipton and Johnson to Pustejovsky and Chen. The marginal RVE/CHE sandwich covariance it targets is precisely the covariance target a Bayesian analogue would have to match, which is the question P2 takes up: across a broad survey of Bayesian meta-analysis, is there any published method that adopts this sandwich covariance as an explicit calibration target?

title: 3  Bayesian meta-analysis & the missing analogue
text:
3.1 3.1 The flexibility is real — on-scope/by-analogy split held explicit
The previous chapter fixed a specific object — the marginal RVE/CHE sandwich covariance of the effect-size vector — and made it the standard any dependent-effect analogue must match. This chapter asks the obvious next question: has the Bayesian meta-analysis literature, in all its breadth, ever targeted that same object as an explicit calibration target? Bayesian meta-analysis is a genuinely flexible, modeling-centric enterprise. It supports weakly informative priors on heterogeneity, invariant priors derived from first principles, hierarchical model-averaging, full multivariate and three-level structures, and covariate-dependent location-scale heterogeneity — and that flexibility is real and well documented. The argument of this chapter is therefore not that the Bayesian tradition is too thin to host a robust-variance analogue. It is the opposite: the tradition is capable across its published breadth, yet across that same breadth no method targets the marginal RVE/CHE sandwich covariance as an explicit calibration target in the frequentist-RVE sense. The two facts are not in tension. The first establishes the second’s character: the absence is one of application, not of Bayesian capacity. This chapter establishes the flexibility first, holding the on-scope/by-analogy distinction explicit so the flexibility is read off the right sources; it then documents the gap as a systematic absence, explains structurally why such an object has not arisen, bounds the near-neighbors that might be mistaken for analogues, and hands forward to the calibrated-Bayes license that would close it.
Bayesian meta-analysis is modeling-centric and genuinely flexible: weakly informative priors on heterogeneity, hierarchical and three-level random-effects, multivariate and correlated-effects structures, and covariate-dependent location-scale heterogeneity. This prior and structural flexibility is real and well documented (CL-P2-01, EMPIRICAL). The claim is positive and load-bearing for the whole chapter: a literature this capable, that has nonetheless not aimed at one specific covariance target, exhibits a gap of application, not of incapacity. The discipline that makes the claim defensible is a split that must stay inside the sentence: genuinely Bayesian flexibility is evidenced by on-scope Bayesian sources, while the broader structural landscape (much of it frequentist) is admitted only by analogy, never recruited as Bayesian-flexibility evidence.
The on-scope Bayesian carriers establish the flexibility directly, each on a different axis. On the prior-modeling axis, Williams, Rast and Bürkner (2018) characterize and validate half-t and inverse-gamma priors for the between-study variance τ², the canonical weakly-informative-prior leg. The half-t / half-Cauchy variance-prior family for hierarchical scale parameters traces to Gelman (2006), and Gelman, Jakulin, Pittau and Su (2008) supply the canonical default weakly-informative (Cauchy) prior on standardized coefficients, cited strictly as documented prior-flexibility evidence rather than as a meta-analysis method. On the invariant-prior axis, Mathur (2025) derives two invariant Jeffreys priors for the meta-analytic location and heterogeneity, with posterior mode, mean and median estimators and central or shortest credible intervals. On the model-structure axis, Bartoš, Maier, Wagenmakers and colleagues (2026) supply hierarchical three-level Bayesian model-averaging across selection-model and PET–PEESE components. And on the location-scale axis, Jansen and colleagues (2026) and Blowes and colleagues (2026) fit double-hierarchical location-scale models in which between-study variances — and, in Jansen and colleagues (2026), the full between-study variance-covariance and correlations — are made covariate-dependent and estimated by weakly informative Bayesian MCMC. Each of these is cited as motivation for the Bayesian approach, and none as evidence that a Bayesian RVE analogue already exists.
The broader multivariate, correlated-effects and network apparatus is likewise mature, primarily in the (largely frequentist) meta-analytic literature, and Bayesian realizations inherit it by analogy. This is the load-bearing part of the split. Jackson, Riley and White (2011) and Jackson, Viechtbauer and van Aert (2024) enumerate multivariate random-effects models with within-study and between-study covariance and multiple estimation routes (REML, method of moments), so they evidence the structural-flexibility landscape by analogy — they are not Bayesian-flexibility instantiations, and they must never be recruited as such. With the split held explicit, the conclusion is positive and precise: this prior and structural flexibility is real and well documented, and it is exactly this established capacity that licenses the chapter to read the absence of a published Bayesian RVE analogue as a matter of application, not of Bayesian incapacity.

title: 3  Bayesian meta-analysis & the missing analogue
section: 3.2 3.2 Why the gap exists, without implying Bayesian deficiency
text:
3.2 3.2 Why the gap exists, without implying Bayesian deficiency
If the flexibility is real, why has the object never been targeted? An absence with no structural explanation invites suspicion that the search merely missed it. Yet the absence admits a structural explanation (CL-P2-03, POSITIONING; G-23). Because the Bayesian analyst retains the posterior as the primary inferential object, the frequentist sandwich, though theoretically motivated, does not by itself supply a named posterior target that a Bayesian workflow can adopt. A posterior calibrated to a specific marginal RVE/CHE sandwich covariance is simply not a standard reporting object in the Bayesian meta-analysis literature. This explains why the gap of §3.5 exists, crediting the posterior-as-primary-object premise rather than attacking the Bayesian paradigm: the convention reports a posterior distribution, its credible intervals and directional probabilities, not a covariance recalibrated to an external sandwich estimand.
The convention is visible across the on-scope literature. Williams, Rast and Bürkner (2018), Mathur (2025) and Jansen and colleagues (2026) each keep the full posterior (the distribution of τ², credible intervals, directional posterior probabilities) as the sole inferential object, and none raises a covariance-calibration target. The calibration object is not absent because it was tried and abandoned, but because the question is never posed. The most direct corroboration is first-party, and it is the kind of concession an adversarial reviewer cannot wave away. Bartoš and colleagues (2026) keep the model-averaged posterior and inclusion Bayes factors primary. Their footnote records that they decline cluster-robust standard errors precisely because no Bayesian counterpart currently exists. A methodological team working at the Bayesian frontier states, in its own words, that the sandwich-calibrated covariance is not an available named posterior target — exactly the convention this section names. The claim here is structural and positioning, which the corpus can at most exemplify; the systematic-absence framing of §3.5 carries the weight (G-23), and the example confirms the convention rather than proving a non-existence.

title: 3  Bayesian meta-analysis & the missing analogue
section: 3.3 3.3 SEM-based meta-analysis: a conditional near-neighbor
text:
3.3 3.3 SEM-based meta-analysis: a conditional near-neighbor
The first hard objection a reviewer would raise is the most specific: surely Cheung’s structural-equation-modeling (SEM) formulation already supplies an inverse-variance equivalent. It does not, in general, and the bound is precise (CL-P2-04, CONDITIONAL; G-26). Cheung’s SEM–inverse-variance equivalence holds only when the Level-1 (transformed) error variance is fixed equal to one, and it does not hold under heteroscedasticity. The equivalence rests on transforming the within-study error so that its variance is exactly one — \(W^{1/2} V_e W^{1/2} = I_k\) in Cheung (2008, Eq. 19) — and, in the definition-variable representation of Cheung (2013, Eq. 11), on treating the within-study sampling covariance as known. Outside that unit-error-variance, V-known regime (precisely the heteroscedastic regimes the frequentist sandwich was built to absorb), the equivalence does not obtain.
SEM-based meta-analysis is therefore a conditional near-neighbor, bounded by its unit-error-variance condition. Within the meta-analytic-SEM (MASEM) lineage the boundary is corroborated from the other side: Bilici and colleagues (2025) frame current MASEM dependence-handling as not uniformly handling within-study dependence. The conditional framing is the whole point and what makes the boundary informative: SEM reaches the neighborhood of a covariance equivalence and stops at the homoscedastic wall, short of the heteroscedasticity-robust, calibration-targeted object of §3.5. It is named here as a non-counterexample that sharpens the conjunction, never as a prior RVE solution or as support for the gap.

title: 3  Bayesian meta-analysis & the missing analogue
section: 3.4 3.4 Multivariate/multilevel: right structure, no sandwich-calibration objective
text:
3.4 3.4 Multivariate/multilevel: right structure, no sandwich-calibration objective
The second hard objection is structural: multivariate and multilevel Bayesian meta-analysis already models within- and between-study dependence directly, so the machinery for a sandwich analogue is in place. The concession here is genuine, and the bound is again precise (CL-P2-05, CONDITIONAL; G-23). Multivariate and multilevel meta-analysis — Jackson, Riley and White (2011), Van den Noortgate and colleagues (2015), Fernández-Castilla and colleagues (2021) — models within- and between-study dependence hierarchically and preserves observation-level design matrices when moderators vary within study. This is the right structural direction — observation-level representation with design matrices preserved is precisely the structure a Bayesian analogue would build on — and the pillar concedes it as such without reservation.
Yet none of these methods pairs that structure with a calibrated-Bayes realignment of the posterior covariance to a frequentist-RVE sandwich target. The structure is present; the sandwich-calibration objective is not. Each of these methods estimates a model-based between-study covariance (by REML, by method of moments, or by its Bayesian counterpart) and reports inference from it. None realigns that covariance toward the marginal RVE/CHE sandwich estimand; for this Bayesian-framed claim, most carriers are moreover frequentist and the structural match holds by analogy. Even the strongest near-neighbor sharpens rather than closes the conjunction. Jansen and colleagues (2026), who let the full between-study variance-covariance matrix depend on covariates, embody about as rich a multivariate Bayesian structure as the literature offers, yet never adopt the sandwich-calibration objective. The richest available structure still leaves the calibration target unoccupied, narrowing the conjunction precisely by occupying so much of the structural neighborhood without occupying the target. Naming the covariance target every time — the marginal RVE/CHE sandwich — the claim is bounded conditionally: structure is part of the right direction; the sandwich-calibration pairing is what is absent. It is never escalated to a non-existence proof.

title: 3  Bayesian meta-analysis & the missing analogue
section: 3.5 3.5 The gap, stated verbatim, and the off-target 2023–2025 frontier
text:
3.5 3.5 The gap, stated verbatim, and the off-target 2023–2025 frontier
Having conceded the flexibility (§3.1), explained the absence (§3.2), and bounded the two nearest structural neighbors (§§3.3–3.4), the chapter states its load-bearing assertion. Surveying the Bayesian meta-analysis literature in full — weakly informative and invariant priors, correlated-effects and correlated-hierarchical-effects (CE/CHE) working models, multivariate and three-level structure, network meta-analysis including 2023–2025 entries, location-scale heterogeneity, SEM-based meta-analysis, and composite-likelihood / Open-Faced-Sandwich near-neighbors — yields one assertion, stated here in its locked wording and nowhere else (CL-P2-02, GAP-ASSERTION; G-23, G-27):

We found no published Bayesian meta-analysis method that targets the RVE/CHE sandwich covariance as an explicit calibration target (in the frequentist-RVE sense), realigned under a Little/Rubin calibrated-Bayes license.

This assertion carries no citation, by design: its evidence is not any single carrier but the breadth of the documented search behind it. The strength of the claim equals the strength of that search, which spans weakly informative priors, CE/CHE working models, multivariate and three-level models, network meta-analysis including 2023–2025 entries, location-scale heterogeneity, SEM-based meta-analysis, and composite-likelihood / Open-Faced-Sandwich near-neighbors. The claim is phrased as the unoccupied conjunction, both qualifiers retained — the RVE/CHE-sandwich target and the Little/Rubin license — and is never escalated to a proof that no such method exists or that the problem has never been formalized. Every individual ingredient (posterior recoloring, a sandwich or Godambe covariance, a calibrated-Bayes license) is already in print; what the search does not find is their conjunction aimed at the marginal RVE/CHE meta-regression target (G-27).
The decisive adversarial pressure on a gap claim is always the most recent stratum, and the 2023–2025 frontier is genuinely active — which is precisely why it must be examined rather than waved past (CL-P2-06, CONDITIONAL; G-23, G-27). The frontier is active, and it is off-target. Wang, Lin and Liu (2025) are the nearest of the recent entries: they recolor a posterior toward a composite-likelihood Godambe sandwich under a Shaby (2014) Open-Faced-Sandwich license anchored at the maximum a posteriori (MAP) estimate. That is a different sandwich (Godambe, not the RVE/CHE meta-regression target), a different license (Open-Faced-Sandwich, not Little/Rubin), and a different anchor (the MAP, not the posterior mean) — three coordinates away from the conjunction the locked sentence names, so the method acknowledges the nearest neighbor without closing the meta-regression RVE gap. The surrounding frontier sits beside it in the same off-target role: Lambardi di San Miniato and colleagues (2021) recolor toward a composite-likelihood Godambe variance under a Ribatet–Miller asymptotic license; Liu and colleagues (2025) and Guolo (2023) are frequentist Godambe-sandwich methods, none under a Little/Rubin license aimed at the RVE/CHE meta-regression target; and the earlier frequentist network-meta reference point, White and colleagues (2012), sits beneath all of these. The frontier is genuinely active, no entry on it hits the target, and network meta-analysis is not presented as closing the meta-regression RVE gap. Each near-neighbor is a non-counterexample that sharpens the conjunction, by its specific mismatch of sandwich, license or anchor; none is tagged as supporting the gap.

title: 3  Bayesian meta-analysis & the missing analogue
section: 3.6 3.6 Hand-forward to P3: what is missing is the license
text:
3.6 3.6 Hand-forward to P3: what is missing is the license
The pattern is now complete, dictating what the next chapter must supply. Bayesian meta-analysis is flexible and modeling-centric (§3.1); the structural direction of its multivariate and multilevel programs is right (§3.4); the marginal RVE/CHE sandwich covariance is simply not yet a named posterior calibration target within it (§§3.2, 3.5); and the nearest programs — SEM-based (§3.3), multivariate/multilevel (§3.4), and the composite-likelihood frontier (§3.5) — supply ingredients without occupying the conjunction. Read adversarially, the chapter has handed the skeptic every near-neighbor in turn, watching each fall short of the same conjunction and qualifier. These establish what the gap is not: not a deficiency of Bayesian capacity, because the capacity is everywhere documented.
What is missing is not Bayesian capacity but a principled warrant for importing a frequentist covariance target into Bayesian inference: a license under which adopting a sandwich covariance as a calibration objective is justified rather than ad hoc. The flexibility is there; the structure is there. What no Bayesian meta-analysis has had is a license that says, in principled terms, why a posterior should be realigned to a frequentist sandwich while its center is preserved. That warrant is the calibrated-Bayes principle of Little (2012) and Rubin (1984), which judges a Bayesian model against frequentist operating characteristics (preserve the center, realign the covariance) and is established prior art rather than anything this review invents. The next chapter develops that principle as the bridge that turns the documented gap of this chapter into a well-posed Bayesian target.
