title: 4  The calibrated-Bayes lens
text:
4.1 4.1 The principle is prior art; only its meta-analytic application is this review’s
The previous chapter documented an absence: across the published breadth of Bayesian meta-analysis, no method targets the marginal RVE/CHE sandwich covariance as an explicit calibration target, and the absence is one of application, not of Bayesian capacity. What is missing is not machinery but a warrant — a principled reason a Bayesian analyst may import a frequentist covariance target into a posterior workflow without abandoning the posterior as the inferential object. This chapter supplies that warrant and, in doing so, draws a careful map of who owns each part of it. It assembles, in order, the license that makes the import principled (Little/Rubin, prior art), the mechanism that executes it (Williams & Savitsky’s survey-scoped Cholesky projection), the mathematical substrate that makes a covariance target well-defined under misspecification (the Godambe / composite-likelihood sandwich), the void that substrate opens but cannot fill, this review’s own exact result that fills it at the unit-weight boundary, and the positioning that closes the gap. Throughout, the load-bearing object is named each time it appears — the marginal RVE/CHE sandwich covariance, over the matched equicorrelation family \(V_j = a_j C_j(\rho)\) — and at every seam the line between borrowed motivation and owned contribution is held: the principle, the mechanism, and the substrate are prior art; the meta-analytic application, the exact finite-sample identity, and the “CCC” name are this review’s.
The first and most important act of attribution is to credit the licensing principle to its sources. The calibrated-Bayes principle — that a Bayesian model is judged against frequentist operating characteristics, preserving the inference center while realigning the covariance — is established prior art in Rubin (1984) and Little (2012), and this review claims neither the principle nor the label that names it (CL-P3-01, CONDITIONAL; G-24). Rubin (1984) supplies the calibration definition and the operating-characteristic machinery — the \(\mathrm{PC}(X)\) apparatus by which a Bayesian procedure is evaluated against its repeated-sampling behavior — and frames the calibrated Bayesian as one whose posterior statements are checked against frequentist performance. Little (2004, 2012) carries the same logic into the survey and official-statistics context, framing Bayesian inference as judged against design-based frequentist operating characteristics, and explicitly crediting the lineage upstream to Box (1980) and Rubin (1984). The principle is thus doubly anchored as prior art: it is established in named sources, and those sources themselves locate it earlier still. The program’s contribution is the application of this principle to one specific object — the marginal RVE/CHE sandwich covariance in meta-analysis — not the principle that licenses the application.
The principle is live prior art, invoked repeatedly in adjacent work, and each downstream invocation confirms it without relocating its credit. Bissiri, Holmes and Walker (2016) calibrate a learning rate so that posterior quantiles align with frequentist intervals, supplying the general Gibbs-Bayes machinery that permits replacing a likelihood target with a loss or estimating-equation target tied to frequentist performance. Frazier, Drovandi and Kohn (2026) operationalize the principle for a Gibbs-posterior coverage target, judging a generalized-Bayes estimator by its sandwich-based frequentist behavior. Mathur (2025) and Williams, Rast and Bürkner (2018) judge Bayesian estimators of the meta-analytic mean and of heterogeneity by frequentist coverage and bias. These are genuine instances of the calibrated-Bayes posture in action; yet none articulates “preserve the center, realign the covariance” for a covariance target in dependent-effect meta-analysis, and each leaves the credit for the principle with Little and Rubin. The “calibrated Bayes” label itself is coined later in that lineage, not by this review; this review inherits both the principle and its name as established vocabulary and adds only the destination it points the principle at.

title: 4  The calibrated-Bayes lens
section: 4.2 4.2 The affine recoloring: preserve the mean, realign the covariance
text:
4.2 4.2 The affine recoloring: preserve the mean, realign the covariance
Under that license, the operation this review needs takes a precise form: an affine recoloring of the posterior draws that leaves the inference center fixed and realigns the marginal covariance toward a frequentist target. The center it preserves is the posterior (Monte-Carlo) mean, and the realignment is toward a named sandwich covariance; this mechanism is motivated by the calibrated-Bayes principle and is the reason covariance-target adoption is justifiable inside a Bayesian workflow at all (CL-P3-02, HEURISTIC; G-21, G-24). The preserve-the-mean half is attested by Little (2004, 2012), whose posterior mean recovers the design-weighted estimator and is the quantity held fixed. The affine-realignment half is attested mechanically by Williams & Savitsky (2021, Eq. 13), whose scale-and-shape map recenters the draws and rescales their spread toward a frequentist sandwich target. The two halves together describe the move: a linear map applied to centered draws, with the mean as the fixed point of the map and the covariance as the object realigned.
The fixed point is the mean, and that choice is load-bearing: an affine map preserves the center only when the centering point is the mean, so the mechanism is mean-anchored by construction, never mode-anchored. The distinction disqualifies an otherwise close relative. Shaby (2014)’s “open-faced sandwich” recoloring is an affine map of the same algebraic family; it realigns a quasi-posterior covariance to a Godambe sandwich while recentering the draws, but anchors at the quasi-Bayes point estimate, the mode of the composite criterion, an asymptotic-sampling device rather than the posterior mean. It is therefore a mode-anchored near-neighbor that this review does not borrow as its mechanism: the calibrated-Bayes center this review preserves is the posterior mean, and the MAP is reserved for an entirely separate likelihood-identity consequence. Naming the anchor correctly keeps the mechanism faithful to the Little–Rubin license, under which the preserved center is the quantity whose frequentist operating characteristics are being calibrated. All such carriers are asymptotic and survey- or generalized-Bayes-scoped; none instantiates the exact finite-sample, mean-anchored identity that §4.5 reserves to this review. The recoloring as a principle is prior art; its exact finite-sample realization is not.

title: 4  The calibrated-Bayes lens
section: 4.3 4.3 The survey-to-Bayes bridge is W&S’s; the meta-analytic leg is this review’s
text:
4.3 4.3 The survey-to-Bayes bridge is W&S’s; the meta-analytic leg is this review’s
The mechanism named in §4.2 has a precise origin, and crediting it correctly means holding it to its scope without letting it travel further than its authors took it — in survey inference, not meta-analysis. Williams & Savitsky (2021) and Savitsky & Williams (2022) established the survey-estimation-to-Bayesian-paradigm bridge through a Cholesky scale-and-shape projection that double-weights both the unit-level likelihood and the random-effect (group) prior; they never mention meta-analysis, and their projection is approximate and asymptotic (CL-P3-03, CONDITIONAL; G-01, G-02, G-07, G-08). Both weight levels must be stated, because the double-weighting is the construction’s defining feature and the discipline that prevents borrowing it as a single-level finite-sample tool. Williams & Savitsky (2021) double-weight the unit pseudo-likelihood, with weights inversely proportional to the inclusion probability, and the PSU-block / group-level resampling. Savitsky & Williams (2022) carry the same weight-exponentiated construction at both the unit-likelihood and the group-prior levels, while explicitly deferring the covariance fix itself to Williams & Savitsky (2021). The bridge is survey machinery at both levels.
The bridge is asymptotic, not finite-sample; its hardest case is shown by simulation rather than proved. Williams & Savitsky (2021) establish the scale-and-shape projection as a large-sample design-theory result, and the clustering-driven rescaling case — the configuration nearest the dependent-effect setting — is demonstrated by their own simulation evidence (the DE5 and PPS3 designs), not by a theorem. A broad survey-side substrate corroborates the projection’s provenance and asymptotic character without touching meta-analysis: the pseudo-posterior foundation of Savitsky & Toth (2016), with survey weights raised into the likelihood exponent and only an asymptotic contraction proved; the informative-sampling and weighted-distribution lineage of Pfeffermann, Krieger and Rinott (1998); and the survey-software realization of Hornby, Williams, Savitsky and Elkasabi (2023), in which the Cholesky square root appears only as an implementation option, not as a named correction. Each is survey inference, asymptotic, and meta-analysis-free. The survey-to-meta-analysis leg is therefore not something Savitsky & Williams did — it is this review’s own extension — and the bridge is cited here strictly for mechanism motivation: it tells this review what map to apply, not that the map is exact, and not that anyone has applied it to dependent effect sizes. The program, not Savitsky & Williams, owns finite-sample exactness; their work is the asymptotic mechanism, never a benchmark for this review’s finite-sample claim.

title: 4  The calibrated-Bayes lens
section: 4.4 4.4 The Godambe substrate names the void; composite likelihood does not fill it
text:
4.4 4.4 The Godambe substrate names the void; composite likelihood does not fill it
A license and a mechanism still leave one question open: under model misspecification, why is a covariance target even well-defined? The answer is the Godambe / estimating-equation substrate, and its credit, too, must be assigned precisely: it explains why a target exists, not that a correction already does. The Godambe sandwich and estimating-equation substrate supplies the asymptotic mathematical justification for treating covariance realignment under misspecification as principled rather than ad hoc (CL-P3-04, HEURISTIC; G-25). Binder (1983) is the survey origin of the design-based estimating-equation sandwich, deriving the robust variance for estimating-equation parameters under a working model that may misspecify structure. Varin (2008) and Varin, Reid and Firth (2011) formalize the composite-likelihood case, defining the Godambe information \(G = H J^{-1} H\) as the correct information matrix when a composite or pseudo-likelihood model is misspecified, and naming \(G^{-1}\) the robust variance, with Varin, Reid and Firth (2011) cautioning that the robust variance “does not guarantee high efficiency.” This substrate establishes why a covariance target is well-defined under misspecification — why realigning a misspecified working model’s covariance toward a sandwich is a principled move and not an arbitrary one. It is cited to license the existence of a target, never as evidence that a correction for dependent meta-analytic effects has already been constructed.
The substrate’s reach stops well short of supplying that correction (CL-P3-05, CONDITIONAL; G-25). Composite likelihood is asymptotic and assumes estimable low-dimensional margins; under unknown within-study correlations — the regime dependent-effect meta-analysis actually inhabits — its operating guarantees degrade, so it motivates, but does not by itself supply, a covariance correction for dependent effects. Varin, Reid and Firth (2011) are explicit that asymptotic normality with \(G^{-1}\) holds for the within-cluster size \(n\) large with the number of clusters \(m\) fixed, that efficiency is application-specific and can be poor, and that consistency under increasing \(m\) is not rigorously established. The generalized-Bayes literature reinforces the same boundary from the other side: Lyddon, Holmes and Walker (2019) show that under misspecification the model-based posterior spread is the wrong asymptotic object and the sandwich is the right one, correcting a scalar loss-scale by information matching rather than realigning a covariance to a named meta-analytic target; and Tanaka (2026) shows that a scalar rate cannot in general match a sandwich shape, so the curvature-versus-sandwich gap remains open for any matrix-valued target. Each of these names the deficiency the substrate exposes; none supplies the fix. The substrate names the void; it does not fill it. What fills it must be exact, finite-sample, and meta-analytic, and that is this review’s own result.

title: 4  The calibrated-Bayes lens
section: 4.5 4.5 The unit-weight boundary: this review’s exact finite-sample identity
text:
4.5 4.5 The unit-weight boundary: this review’s exact finite-sample identity
The void the substrate opens is filled by this review’s own exact result, cited here motivationally and never re-proved. Here the attribution map turns from borrowed to owned (CL-P3-06, THEOREM; G-08, G-16, G-17). At the unit-weight boundary — where the unit-level weight \(w_{ij} = 1\) and the random-effect (group) weight \(w_\theta = 1\) — a posterior affinely recolored by the marginal RVE sandwich achieves an exact finite-sample identity between the posterior’s marginal covariance and the RVE target, over the matched equicorrelation family \(V_j = a_j C_j(\rho)\). The companion manuscripts pin this result precisely, and their numbering must not be conflated. A companion manuscript (Theorems 1–2 and Corollary 1) identifies the unit-weight bridge, the marginal fixed-effect sandwich target, and the center-preserving recoloring; a companion technical report (Theorems 1–2) supplies the generic CCC center-preservation and the exact covariance-matching algebra. Both are cross-references to companion manuscripts, not bibliography entries, and the chapter motivates the method without re-deriving it.
Three scope facts make this the chapter’s hinge, each a precondition, not a concession. First, the unit-weight boundary is the exact theoretical result and this review’s contribution, the configuration at which calibration is mathematically exact, not a limitation imposed for tractability; the nonuniform-weight case is scoped-out future extension, framed as a precondition for the stated theorem scope rather than as an apology (G-16). Second, its scope is the matched equicorrelation family \(V_j = a_j C_j(\rho)\) with arbitrary per-cluster scale \(a_j > 0\), never arbitrary heteroscedastic or arbitrary positive-definite \(V_j\), which is explicitly outside the proven scope (G-17). Third, this finite-sample exactness is distinct in kind from the asymptotic Williams & Savitsky mechanism: theirs is a large-sample projection, whereas this review’s unit-weight result is an algebraic equality that holds at any sample size within scope, so the asymptotic mechanism must never stand in for this review’s exactness (G-08).
Published near-neighbors sharpen the boundary by lacking it. Williams & Savitsky (2021) supply only the asymptotic scale-and-shape mechanism and state explicitly, in their own §7, that the projection does not force the pseudo-posterior variance to equal the pseudo-MLE’s in small-to-moderate samples, the asymptotic disclaimer that this review’s finite-sample identity answers, and the reason a mechanism that declines the small-sample claim cannot pre-empt it. Lee (2026) achieves, for a single survey, a design-consistent empirical sandwich match through a Cholesky affine transformation, but it is a finite-sample-imperfect match toward an empirical sandwich, not an exact finite-sample identity for the meta-analytic target. Neither occupies this review’s exact finite-sample, unit-weight-boundary result, and the contrast locates exactly what is this review’s own: not the recoloring move, not the asymptotic mechanism, but the exact finite-sample identity at the unit-weight boundary. (What this identity delivers is exact covariance-matching, an algebraic property; coverage is a separate, asymptotic-in-\(J\), simulation-shown operating characteristic, and the affirmative matching-versus-coverage statement is hosted at Chapter 6, kept in its own sentences there [G-09].)

title: 4  The calibrated-Bayes lens
section: 4.6 4.6 CCC is the coinage; blockwise by design; \(\rho\) is conditional
text:
4.6 4.6 CCC is the coinage; blockwise by design; \(\rho\) is conditional
With the exact result in hand, this review’s operationalization acquires a name, a structure, and a working-model parameter. Each is an attribution checkpoint where this review’s coinage must be separated from the borrowed mechanism. CCC (Cholesky Calibration Correction) is an original coinage for the meta-analytic operationalization of the calibrated-Bayes principle; it implements the Williams & Savitsky (2021) scale-and-shape (Cholesky) projection mechanism, with the mechanism attributed to Williams & Savitsky and the name plus the meta-analytic instantiation original to this review (CL-P3-07, POSITIONING; G-06). Williams & Savitsky (2021) supply the Cholesky \(R_2^{-1} R_1\) projection that CCC operationalizes but never use the name “CCC.” The survey-side antecedents (Savitsky & Williams (2022), Savitsky, León-Novelo and Engle (2024), and Hornby, Williams, Savitsky and Elkasabi (2023)) anchor the mechanism’s survey provenance while coining no such label and supplying no meta-analytic instantiation. CCC implements the W&S mechanism; CCC is not from, introduced by, or due to Savitsky or Williams, and it is not a known or established survey technique.
The calibration is blockwise, and this structure is a design choice grounded in geometry, not a workaround. CCC calibration is blockwise by design (a \(\beta\)-block Cholesky on the marginal sandwich target and a \(\theta\)-block diagonal correlated-effects rescaling) because the full-joint \((\beta, \theta)\) sandwich is structurally rank-deficient (rank \(\le J < K + J\), reflecting incidental-parameter geometry), so full-joint Cholesky calibration is not attempted (CL-P3-08, CONDITIONAL, PRIMARY; G-10, G-11). Rank-deficiency disqualifies the full-joint Cholesky; it does not by itself justify the marginal target. The marginal target is justified separately by the estimand: the reported quantity integrates over the random intercepts \(\theta_j\), so the reported covariance must be marginal. The two arguments are kept apart (rank-deficiency for what the full-joint cannot be, the estimand for what the reported covariance must be), and this separation is the discipline (the rank-deficiency result is established in a companion technical report, cited motivationally). Blockwise calibration is the principled realization of these two facts, not a compromise forced by either, and keeping the two apart is what prevents the contribution from reading as a tractability concession.
The working-model correlation parameter is the final attribution checkpoint, because it is the place a reader is most likely to import the wrong object. In the CCC working model, \(\rho\) is the conditional residual (Level-1) working correlation of \(y_j\) given \((\beta, \theta_j)\) — the within-cluster correlation before \(\theta_j\) is integrated out — and it is not the marginal intra-class correlation, which is inflated by \(\tau^2\) through \(\Sigma_j(\tau^2) = V_j + \tau^2\,\mathbf{1}\mathbf{1}^\top\) (CL-P3-10, CONDITIONAL, PRIMARY; G-19, G-20). The distinction is load-bearing: \(\rho\) parameterizes within-cluster Level-1 dependence, while the marginal correlation an analyst might mistake it for is the \(\tau^2\)-inflated quantity. The fixed-\(\rho\) / equicorrelation specification is a working-model and reporting-scope choice; it is not the logical site at which exactness arises, which lives instead in matching the known within-cluster covariance structure (the \(\rho\)-definition is established in a companion technical report, cited motivationally). The fixed-\(\rho\) boundary is a precondition for the stated theorem scope, never the mechanism that produces the exact identity. Framing it as scope rather than as the source of exactness keeps the §4.5 result from reading as a narrow curiosity about a fixed correlation rather than what it is: an exact match to a known covariance structure.

title: 4  The calibrated-Bayes lens
section: 4.7 4.7 No prior Bayesian meta-analysis aimed the principle at the RVE/CHE sandwich
text:
4.7 4.7 No prior Bayesian meta-analysis aimed the principle at the RVE/CHE sandwich
The attribution map now closes on the positioning claim. With the principle credited to Little and Rubin (§4.1), the mechanism to Williams & Savitsky (§4.3), the substrate to Binder and Varin (§4.4), and the exact identity and CCC name owned by this review (§4.5–4.6), the gap is stated: we found no prior Bayesian meta-analysis paper that has applied the calibrated-Bayes principle — preserve the center, realign the covariance — to the RVE/CHE sandwich covariance as an explicit calibration target, so this bridge from calibrated-Bayes philosophy to dependent-effect meta-analysis is this review’s contribution (CL-P3-09, POSITIONING, PRIMARY; G-01, G-23, G-24, G-27). This is the positioning counterpart of the verbatim negative-existence claim, which is single-hosted at §3.5 of the previous chapter and is not restated here. The principle is prior art (Little (2012); Rubin (1984)); Savitsky & Williams remain survey-scoped; and the recent near-neighbors realign toward composite-likelihood Godambe sandwiches under licenses other than Little–Rubin. The strength of the positioning rests, as the negative does, on a documented systematic absence and is phrased conditionally; it is never escalated to a claim that no such method could exist.
The discipline that makes the positioning precise is the combinatorial-gap discipline: every individual ingredient of the conjunction already exists in print, so the gap can only be the unoccupied conjunction, never priority over any single ingredient (G-27). Each near-neighbor instantiates exactly one ingredient (posterior recoloring toward some sandwich in a non-meta-analytic setting) and stops there. Müller (2013) recolors a posterior toward the sandwich in misspecified econometric and time-series likelihoods, preserving the center and realigning the covariance, but never in meta-analysis. Shaby (2014) performs the same move in spatial and extreme-value GLMs, toward a composite-likelihood Godambe target under an asymptotic license and at the mode, not the meta-analytic RVE/CHE sandwich. Frazier, Drovandi and Kohn (2026), Lyddon, Holmes and Walker (2019), and Tanaka (2026) each realign a generalized-Bayes posterior toward a sandwich-flavored target on independent data, in several cases tuning only a scalar, with no meta-analysis and no Little/Rubin license. Lambardi di San Miniato and Sartori (2021) match curvature toward a robust sandwich under composite-likelihood asymptotics, again outside meta-analysis. Luo and Ji (2026) recolor uncertainty for single-dataset functionals; Wang, Lin and Liu (2025) recolor at the MAP under a Shaby open-faced-sandwich license toward a composite-likelihood Godambe target; and Bissiri, Holmes and Walker (2016) supply the enabling Gibbs-Bayes machinery that permits swapping a likelihood target for a loss target without ever performing it for the meta-analytic target. Each is a single ingredient, present in print, and each — by realigning toward some sandwich in some non-meta-analytic setting — confirms rather than occupies the conjunction. The principle, the mechanism, and the substrate are borrowed; the assembly — the calibrated-Bayes license, the marginal RVE/CHE sandwich as the named target, and the dependent-effect meta-analytic setting, all at once — is the unoccupied site this review fills.
That assembly answers why a covariance target may be imported into a Bayesian meta-analysis at all. It does not yet answer why one would want a specifically Bayesian robust variance estimator rather than the frequentist sandwich already in hand. P3 hands forward to P4: why a Bayesian RVE specifically (the predictor-specific diagnostics, the design-effect-ratio exemplar, and the operating-envelope advantages the gap demands over the frequentist all-or-nothing sandwich), with covariance-matching (exact, algebraic) and coverage (asymptotic, simulation-shown) held strictly apart throughout.

title: 5  Why Bayesian RVE specifically
text:
5.1 5.1 The three capabilities, as positioning (not coverage guarantees)
The previous chapter answered why a covariance target may be imported into a Bayesian meta-analysis at all: the calibrated-Bayes license (Little/Rubin), the survey-scoped Cholesky mechanism (Williams & Savitsky), and this review’s exact unit-weight-boundary identity together vacate the gap that the substrate opens. What they do not yet answer is why one would want a specifically Bayesian robust variance estimator rather than the frequentist sandwich already in hand. This chapter answers that question. The answer is deliberately not “because it covers better.” A Bayesian RVE earns its place by supplying three modeling capabilities the frequentist construction does not readily afford: flexible prior modeling of between-study heterogeneity, preservation of observation-level multilevel structure where aggregation fails, and per-coefficient design-effect-ratio diagnostics. Each is stated below as positioning (what the paradigm can do) and held rigorously from sliding into a statement about how an interval behaves. The chapter then records this review’s exact covariance-matching results, cited motivationally, and closes by stating, once and verbatim, the second gap this review occupies. Throughout, the load-bearing object is named each time it appears (the marginal RVE/CHE sandwich covariance), and the frequentist contrast is held neutral: a difference of design class, never a defect.
The case opens with the three capabilities themselves, framed at the outset as what the Bayesian paradigm affords. A specifically Bayesian formulation of robust variance estimation offers three things frequentist RVE does not readily provide: flexible prior modeling of between-study heterogeneity, preservation of observation-level multilevel structure, and predictor-specific (per-coefficient) diagnostics. These are positioning advantages, not coverage guarantees, each a modeling capability rather than a claim about the operating characteristics of any estimator (CL-P4-01, POSITIONING). The first two capabilities are well-attested in the adjacent literature. Williams, Rast and Bürkner (2018) demonstrate the prior-modeling flexibility a Bayesian heterogeneity model affords: weakly informative and structured priors on the between-study variance, with the heterogeneity parameter treated as a first-class modeled quantity rather than a plug-in. Van den Noortgate, López-López, Marín-Martínez and Sánchez-Meca (2015) and Fernández-Castilla, Jamshidi, Declercq, Beretvas, Onghena and Van den Noortgate (2021) supply the observation-level multilevel structure, in which the within-study design matrix is preserved at the level of the individual effect size. These three carriers establish advantages (i) and (ii), and only those two: none pairs the structure with an RVE/CHE sandwich-calibration target, and none is credited with the diagnostic advantage (iii), which is carried separately below.
The evidentiary posture here is exemplary motivation, not demonstrated superiority, and the bar is exactly that. No on-scope, dependent-effect Bayesian meta-analytic carrier instantiates all three advantages at once; the first two are carried by near-neighbor exemplars from the adjacent survey and small-area-estimation literature, where Rao and Molina (2015) document that flexible Bayesian small-area methods and unit-level multilevel structure are a mature, well-established toolkit. The third advantage, the per-coefficient diagnostic, is the design-effect-ratio diagnostic, survey-scope in origin (Lee (2026b), arXiv:2603.07791; cross-referenced to §5.3) and developed for meta-analytic dependent effects in a forthcoming companion paper, not here. Each near-neighbor instantiates at most two of the three advantages, never in a dependent-effect meta-analytic frame paired with a sandwich-calibration target, and that is precisely the bar the corpus is asked to meet.
One of the three, prior flexibility, also opens a forward door the review acknowledges but does not walk through. Flexible Bayesian prior modeling of heterogeneity is a natural launchpad for Bayesian network-meta-analysis extensions, a downstream use case this review acknowledges but does not undertake (CL-P4-02, CONDITIONAL). The pointer is deliberately conditional: the network-meta-analysis development lies outside this review, no coverage or finite-sample property is attached to it, and the topically nearest corpus entries operate by the wrong mechanism (Wang, Lin and Liu (2025) recolor toward a composite-likelihood Godambe target under a non-informative-prior Open-Faced-Sandwich license, and Liu and colleagues (2025) work frequentially), so neither is read as a supporting carrier. The launchpad is named as a possibility, not promised as a result.

title: 5  Why Bayesian RVE specifically
section: 5.2 5.2 Observation-level structure where aggregation fails
text:
5.2 5.2 Observation-level structure where aggregation fails
The second capability is the Bayesian realization of a design-class distinction the frequentist chapter already established, and the direction of that distinction is load-bearing. The unaggregated, observation-level Bayesian likelihood preserves within-study design matrices even when moderators vary within study (Van Den Noortgate et al., 2015): the regime in which the frequentist aggregated, one-row-per-study representation ceases to exist while the correlated-effects model remains well-defined (Pustejovsky and Chen 2024, cited motivation) (CL-P4-03, CONDITIONAL; G-05). The direction is held fixed: it is the aggregated representation that breaks, not the observation-level model. This is the same design-class distinction fixed for frequentist RVE in Chapter 2 (CL-P1-06), now realized in a Bayesian likelihood: the unaggregated likelihood survives precisely where aggregation cannot express the within-study structure.
The corpus corroborates the structural half of this claim across both survey and frequentist meta-analytic neighbors, while the directional half rests on cited analytic motivation. Van den Noortgate et al. (2015) supply the one-row-per-effect-size observation-level likelihood that keeps the within-study design matrix intact, though in a frequentist REML frame and paired with a single equicorrelation variance component rather than a sandwich-calibration target. Song et al. (2020) exhibit a genuine aggregation-failure mode: a non-linear combined outcome cannot be expressed at the aggregated study level, so observation-level reconstruction is required to represent it at all. Neither carrier states the directional result that the aggregated representation is the side that ceases to exist; that direction is Pustejovsky and Chen’s (2024) analytic distinction, cited motivationally, not a corpus finding.
The within-study working model that makes this structure operational is not re-derived here; it is borrowed as substrate from the calibrated-Bayes chapter. The CCC calibration carried into this regime is blockwise by design (a \(\beta\)-block Cholesky on the marginal sandwich target and a \(\theta\)-block diagonal correlated-effects rescaling), so the observation-level structure is calibrated without attempting a full-joint Cholesky on a structurally rank-deficient sandwich (reprise of CL-P3-08, primary at §4.6; G-10, G-11). And the within-study correlation that working model carries is \(\rho\), the conditional residual Level-1 working correlation of \(y_j\) given \((\beta, \theta_j)\), not the marginal intra-class correlation, which is \(\tau^2\)-inflated through \(\Sigma_j(\tau^2) = V_j + \tau^2\,\mathbf{1}\mathbf{1}^\top\) (reprise of CL-P3-10, primary at §4.6; G-19, G-20). Both objects are used here as the ground on which observation-level structure stands; their full statement lives at §4.6 and is not re-hosted. What this section adds is only that the observation-level likelihood is the regime in which that working model has something to preserve — the within-study design matrix the aggregated representation cannot hold.

title: 5  Why Bayesian RVE specifically
section: 5.3 5.3 Per-coefficient DER diagnostics: selective vs all-or-nothing
text:
5.3 5.3 Per-coefficient DER diagnostics: selective vs all-or-nothing
The third capability is the one that most sharply distinguishes a Bayesian RVE, and it is the diagnostic the chapter foregrounds. A per-coefficient design-effect-ratio diagnostic identifies which coefficients require variance correction, enabling selective, predictor-specific correction rather than the all-or-nothing application that the frequentist sandwich provides (CL-P4-04, POSITIONING; G-22). The contrast is a design-class difference, framed neutrally: the frequentist sandwich applies one robust covariance to the whole coefficient vector, by construction; a per-coefficient ratio resolves the correction to the coefficient, telling the analyst that, say, the intercept is well-served by the model-based posterior while a moderator slope is materially under-dispersed. This is an added resolution the Bayesian construction affords, not a deficiency it exposes in the sandwich.
The diagnostic’s origin must be named precisely, because the line between its established scope and its future scope is exactly the chapter’s G-22 discipline. The design-effect-ratio diagnostic is established for Bayesian survey models by Lee (2026b), where a per-coefficient ratio of the design-corrected sandwich variance to the MCMC posterior variance identifies which coefficients are survey-sensitive and need correction. Its meta-analytic, per-coefficient extension to dependent effects is developed in a forthcoming companion paper, not anticipated here: Lee (2026b) performs no meta-analysis, and the dependent-effect version is the forward contribution this review motivates rather than pre-empts. The diagnostic is therefore cited as a survey-scope origin and exemplar, never as a meta-analytic result already in hand.
Two bounded near-neighbors fix what the diagnostic is by showing what it is not, and neither is a meta-analytic DER. Giordano and Broderick (2024) are the named diagnostic, non-corrective neighbor: they compute a frequentist sandwich variance from a single MCMC run, with per-parameter inconsistency governed diagnostically by how tightly each parameter’s posterior concentrates. They show only that Bayesian draws can yield Bayesian-versus-frequentist per-parameter variance comparisons, not a per-coefficient design-effect ratio for dependent meta-analytic effects. Van Lissa, van Erp and Clapper (2023), in Bayesian regularized meta-regression, supply the per-coefficient moderator-selection ingredient by coefficient-magnitude shrinkage (LASSO/horseshoe priors with per-coefficient inclusion), which selects moderators coefficient-by-coefficient but is not a robust-versus-model variance ratio and so does not flag which coefficients need variance correction. The classical multivariate design-effect lineage positions the diagnostic within an established tradition without anticipating it: Hornby et al. (2023) and Williams, McGuire and Savitsky (2025) cast the \(R_2^{-1} R_1\) projection as a parameter-specific design effect in the Kish sense, a survey design effect rather than the meta-analytic per-coefficient ratio. Every carrier is survey-scoped, non-corrective, or magnitude-selection; the meta-analytic per-coefficient DER is, by design, a forthcoming companion paper’s territory.

title: 5  Why Bayesian RVE specifically
section: 5.4 5.4 The MCMC-vs-CCC comparison as a forward-pointing diagnostic
text:
5.4 5.4 The MCMC-vs-CCC comparison as a forward-pointing diagnostic
The diagnostic acquires its meta-analytic shape as a comparison the review motivates and explicitly hands forward. The MCMC-versus-CCC design-effect-ratio comparison is a data-driven diagnostic for identifying correction-sensitive parameters in meta-analytic dependent-effect inference; this review motivates the diagnostic, drawing on the Bayesian survey-scope design-effect-ratio of Lee (2026b), and points to a forthcoming companion paper, which develops it for dependent effects (CL-P4-05, POSITIONING; G-22). The single survey-scope precedent (Lee (2026b), contrasting the MCMC posterior variance against a design-corrected sandwich target) establishes the comparison as a data-driven diagnostic for correction-sensitive parameters, but performs no meta-analysis; the dependent-effect version is forward-pointing positioning the review motivates without pre-empting. The diagnostic asserts no finite-sample or coverage property of its own; it is a reading of where the two covariances diverge.
What already exists in this review’s software must be stated accurately, because the deferral to a forthcoming companion paper is a deferral of a workflow, not an admission that the diagnostic machinery is missing. Current bayesRVE outputs already include scoped DER-adjacent quantities (DER_mu for the intercept, the \(\theta\) summaries, the CCC scale factors r_k, and the CHE diagonal DER_emp), so the diagnostic ingredient is present and computed, not absent. These outputs are narrower than a forthcoming companion paper’s full contrast-level / default DER workflow for pre-specified contrasts with named variance targets: they are intercept- and diagonal-scoped diagnostics, where a forthcoming companion paper develops the general per-contrast, per-coefficient reporting workflow. The accurate statement is therefore one of scope, not of absence — the current outputs expose a restricted DER-adjacent diagnostic, and the full meta-analytic per-coefficient development is a forthcoming companion paper’s.

title: 5  Why Bayesian RVE specifically
section: 5.5 5.5 The program’s matching results (motivational) and matching is not coverage
text:
5.5 5.5 The program’s matching results (motivational) and matching is not coverage
This is the chapter’s hinge, and it states two things in a fixed order: first what this review proves exactly, then what it does not. The three results below are cited motivationally and are never re-proved here; they appear as prose cross-references to the companion manuscripts, with their numbering pinned exactly so that the conditional bridge, the marginal identity, and the correct-specification result are never conflated.
The first result is the conditional bridge. A companion manuscript, Theorem 1 (the Clusterwise Likelihood Identity), establishes that, at the unit-weight boundary under the matched equicorrelation family \(V_j = a_j C_j(\rho)\), the meta-analytic and survey interpretations assign the same clusterwise conditional Gaussian likelihood, hence the same likelihood-derived scores and curvature (CL-P4-07, THEOREM; G-16, G-17). The Cholesky-affine covariance-matching step that reports the target RVE sandwich is supplied by a companion technical report, Theorem 2, together with the companion manuscript’s Corollary 1. The unit-weight boundary is the exact theoretical result and this review’s contribution, not a limitation imposed for tractability (G-16), and the scope is the matched equicorrelation family \(V_j = a_j C_j(\rho)\) — never arbitrary heteroscedastic or arbitrary positive-definite \(V_j\) (G-17).
The second result is strictly more general. A companion manuscript, Theorem 2 (the Marginal Fixed-Effect Sandwich Identity), establishes that, under unit weights and the matched translation, the meta-analytic and survey interpretations have the same marginal fixed-effect sandwich target after integrating out the random intercepts \(\theta_j\) (CL-P4-08, THEOREM; G-18). This marginal argument is strictly more general than the conditional / equicorrelation bridge because it rests on equality of matched marginal Gaussian densities (\(\ell_{\text{surv}} = \ell_{\text{meta}} + c\)) with no compound-symmetric requirement; the affine posterior recoloring to that target is the companion manuscript’s Corollary 1 and the generic covariance-matching algebra of the companion technical report, Theorem 2. The two scopes are not flattened: the marginal identity dominates the conditional one in generality, and the asymmetry is preserved. The third result is a neutrality check. A companion technical report, Theorem 3 (Identity Under Correct Specification), establishes that when the MCMC posterior covariance already equals the target covariance, CCC is the identity transformation: the correction is neutral when the working model is right (CL-P4-09, THEOREM; G-12). This neutrality holds conditional on the plug-in \(\omega^2\) and the within-study correlation; if \(\omega^2\) is badly misspecified the target itself is wrong, and no data-driven \(\omega^2\)-misspecification correction is provided. (The companion manuscript’s Corollary 1 is the recoloring result, not this neutrality theorem, and the two are not interchanged.)
Now the spine of the chapter, stated in two sentences that are never bridged. Covariance-matching, as established by the three CCC theorems above, is an exact finite-sample algebraic property of the calibrated posterior’s covariance relative to the named marginal RVE/CHE sandwich target (CL-P4-10, EMPIRICAL; G-09, G-13). Coverage is a separate operating characteristic: it is asymptotic in \(J\) and demonstrated only by simulation under specified data-generating processes (program simulation evidence, TSL15 / sim3), where the calibrated intervals are near nominal. The two claims are distinct. Exact algebraic matching does not by itself imply nominal interval coverage, and “matches exactly” is never permitted to slide into “ensures coverage.” The simulation evidence is proof-of-concept under the conditions studied, not universal; multivariate coefficient-level behavior remains open, and no specific coverage percentage is cited (G-13). What the theorems give is an algebraic identity; what the simulation gives is an operating characteristic; the contribution is that the first holds exactly and the second is shown, separately, to be near nominal.

title: 5  Why Bayesian RVE specifically
section: 5.6 5.6 The second gap, stated verbatim
text:
5.6 5.6 The second gap, stated verbatim
All three ingredients of a Bayesian RVE with per-coefficient diagnostics now exist in print (MCMC-based Bayesian inference, the design-effect-ratio diagnostic, and the dependent-effect meta-analytic setting), yet the documented synthesis-level sweep across the Bayesian meta-analysis, design-effect / small-area, and Bayesian survey literatures finds the conjunction of all three unoccupied. The gap is therefore combinatorial, and it is stated in this review’s locked wording, reached through the breadth of that search rather than from any single citation (CL-P4-06, GAP-ASSERTION, PRIMARY HOST; G-22, G-23, G-27):

We found no published method that combines MCMC-based Bayesian inference with per-coefficient design-effect-ratio diagnostics for meta-analytic dependent effect sizes.

The strength of the assertion equals the strength of the documented search behind it; it is never escalated to “no such method exists” or to a priority claim over any single ingredient. Every individual ingredient already exists in print (MCMC-based Bayesian inference, design-effect ratios, per-coefficient diagnostics), but the conjunction is unoccupied for the meta-analytic dependent-effect setting. The named near-neighbors are non-counterexamples that sharpen the conjunction rather than close it, and none may be tagged as supporting it. Lee (2026a) instantiates the Cholesky-sandwich-plus-per-coefficient-DER conjunction, but only at survey scope. Lee (2026b) supplies the per-coefficient DER ingredient for Bayesian survey models, with the word “meta-analysis” absent. Van Lissa, van Erp and Clapper (2023) supply per-coefficient moderator selection by coefficient-magnitude shrinkage, not a robust-versus-model variance ratio. Frazier, Drovandi and Kohn (2026) provide neither a robust-versus-model variance ratio nor per-coefficient targeting. Each instantiates one ingredient and stops there; the conjunction — MCMC-based inference, a per-coefficient DER diagnostic, and the dependent-effect meta-analytic setting, all at once — is the unoccupied site, and the meta-analytic per-coefficient diagnostic that would fill it remains the scope of a forthcoming companion paper.
Taken together, the four pillars now close the funnel. P1 fixed the frequentist RVE standard and the design-class distinction; P2 documented the absence of a published Bayesian RVE analogue targeting the RVE/CHE sandwich; P3 supplied the calibrated-Bayes license that authorizes recoloring a posterior toward that sandwich; and P4 has shown what a specifically Bayesian RVE adds (flexible heterogeneity priors, observation-level structure where aggregation fails, and per-coefficient DER diagnostics the frequentist construction does not readily afford) as positioning advantages, with covariance-matching (exact, algebraic) and coverage (asymptotic, simulation-shown) held strictly apart. These threads now hand to the consolidated gap-and-contribution synthesis, where the unoccupied conjunction, this review’s matching results, and the matching-versus-coverage distinction are assembled into this review’s stated contribution and its deferral of the meta-analytic per-coefficient diagnostic to a forthcoming companion paper.

title: 6  The gap, the contribution & claim-type discipline
text:
6.1 6.1 Claim-type discipline as a reading key
The four pillars are now in place. Chapter 2 established that the frequentist robust-variance-estimation (RVE) lineage owns dependent-effect meta-analysis and fixed the marginal RVE/CHE sandwich covariance as the target any analogue must match. Chapter 3 documented that the Bayesian meta-analysis literature, for all its modeling flexibility, left that target unoccupied as a named posterior calibration object. Chapter 4 credited the calibrated-Bayes principle of Little and Rubin with the license to import the target, located the Williams and Savitsky Cholesky mechanism that operationalizes it, and named this review’s exact unit-weight-boundary identity. Chapter 5 made the affirmative case for a specifically Bayesian RVE and recorded this review’s matching results, holding matching and coverage apart. This chapter adds no fifth argument and asserts no new gap. It is the join: it supplies the reader with the claim-type key used throughout the book (§6.1), recalls the two documented gaps as twin anchors (§6.2), states this review’s affirmative contribution that occupies both (§6.3), and sorts the result into what is owned, what is borrowed, and what is deferred (§6.4). Nothing here is asserted for the first time except the affirmative contribution statement itself.
Every load-bearing claim in this book carries a type, and the type is not decoration — it is a contract about evidence. The taxonomy is the key the reader has been using, often tacitly, across all five preceding chapters; stating it plainly here lets the convergence that follows be read precisely rather than impressionistically. A reviewer can read each tag as an instruction for what to demand. A THEOREM is an exact established result of the program, and of this review only; it is cited motivationally with its source and scope, never re-proved here. A result of the frequentist RVE field is therefore never typed THEOREM but rather CONDITIONAL, POSITIONING, or EMPIRICAL, because the label tracks ownership, not mere mathematical truth. A GAP-ASSERTION is a negative claim with warrant in documented systematic absence across an exhaustive search, never a single citation; phrased as “we found no published method that…,” never as an unconditional non-existence proof. A CONDITIONAL binds a claim to its condition in the same breath — “under [stated condition], [claim]” — and is never read as universal. A POSITIONING claim frames a contribution or a relation, asserted directly but with each component attributed to its owner. An EMPIRICAL claim rests on data or simulation, and its source must be named; bare figures are banned, and a qualitative reading (“near nominal”) stands in until an exact number is verified against its source.
Two rules ride on top of the taxonomy, and together they are what make the funnel auditable. The first is the name-the-target rule: wherever the book touches calibration, a sandwich, or RVE covariance, it names the specific object (the marginal CR2/RVE sandwich covariance for the fixed-effect block, the matched equicorrelation family \(V_j = a_j C_j(\rho)\), the conditional versus the marginal covariance) because the argument moves between frequentist and Bayesian idioms and an unnamed “sandwich” invites misreading. The second is the matching-versus-coverage separation: covariance-matching (exact, finite-sample, algebraic) and coverage (asymptotic in \(J\), simulation-shown) are different types of claim living in different sentences wherever both appear, ensuring an algebraic identity is never quietly upgraded into an operating-characteristic guarantee. The calibrated-Bayes principle that licenses the whole construction is itself credited as prior art, not as a program invention, every time it is named. The payoff of the key is traceability: because each claim wears its type, its source, and (where the principle is borrowed) its attribution, a reader can stop at any sentence and reconstruct what would have to be true for it to hold and who would have to be cited to support it. The convergence of §6.2 and §6.3 is legible only because every element arrives pre-typed; §6.4 applies the key exhaustively to the finished contribution.

title: 6  The gap, the contribution & claim-type discipline
section: 6.2 6.2 The two unoccupied conjunctions restated
text:
6.2 6.2 The two unoccupied conjunctions restated
The funnel’s two negative findings are recalled here side by side as the twin anchors of the join. Neither is established in this chapter. Each was documented at its full search breadth at its primary host upstream and is recalled below verbatim (exactly as locked) so the affirmative contribution in §6.3 can be read against the precise absences it occupies. The strength of each remains the strength of the documented search that produced it; nothing here adds to it.
The first anchor is the gap documented in Chapter 3 (§3.5) across the breadth of the Bayesian meta-analysis literature — weakly informative priors, correlated- and correlated-hierarchical-effects working models, multivariate and three-level models, network meta-analysis including 2023–2025 entries, location-scale heterogeneity, structural-equation-model-based meta-analysis, and composite-likelihood / Open-Faced-Sandwich near-neighbors. Recalled here word for word (REPRISE CL-P2-02, primary at §3.5):

We found no published Bayesian meta-analysis method that targets the RVE/CHE sandwich covariance as an explicit calibration target (in the frequentist-RVE sense), realigned under a Little/Rubin calibrated-Bayes license.

The second anchor is the gap documented in Chapter 5 (§5.6) across the Bayesian meta-analysis, design-effect / small-area, and Bayesian survey literatures, where the conjunction of MCMC-based inference, a per-coefficient design-effect-ratio diagnostic, and the dependent-effect meta-analytic setting was found unoccupied. Recalled here word for word (REPRISE CL-P4-06, primary at §5.6):

We found no published method that combines MCMC-based Bayesian inference with per-coefficient design-effect-ratio diagnostics for meta-analytic dependent effect sizes.

Set side by side, the two sentences are reprises and nothing more. Each is combinatorial: every individual ingredient already exists in print (posterior recoloring, the marginal sandwich target, the calibrated-Bayes license, design-effect ratios, per-coefficient moderator selection), so each gap is only the unoccupied conjunction of those ingredients, never a priority claim over any single one (G-27). Neither is re-tagged as a fresh GAP-ASSERTION here, and neither is ever escalated to “no such method exists” (G-23). The near-neighbors that sharpen them are recalled in the same spirit, as non-counterexamples already adjudicated at their primary hosts. Wang, Lin and Liu (2025) recolor toward a composite-likelihood Godambe target under an Open-Faced-Sandwich license anchored at the MAP — a different sandwich, a different license, a different anchor — and so sharpen the first conjunction without occupying it. Lee (2026a) instantiates the Cholesky-sandwich-plus-per-coefficient-diagnostic move, but only at survey scope for a single survey, and so sharpens the second without performing the meta-analytic, dependent-effect version (G-22). Each near-neighbor confirms, rather than closes, its conjunction.
Standing beside the two recalled negatives is their positioning counterpart, also recalled from Chapter 4 (§4.7): no prior Bayesian meta-analysis aimed the calibrated-Bayes principle — preserve the center, realign the covariance — at the RVE/CHE sandwich covariance as an explicit calibration target (REPRISE CL-P3-09, primary at §4.7). This is the affirmative-facing restatement of the same documented absence: where CL-P2-02 records that no Bayesian method occupies the target, CL-P3-09 records that no Bayesian paper aimed the principle at it, and the two are the negative and positioning faces of one finding, both hosted upstream. With the target named and owned (P1), the conjunction empirically unoccupied (P2 and P4), and the licensing principle established prior art that no one had yet aimed at this target (P3), the site this review fills is exactly located. Exactly one move closes it.

title: 6  The gap, the contribution & claim-type discipline
section: 6.3 6.3 CCC fills the gap: exact matching, coverage held separate
text:
6.3 6.3 CCC fills the gap: exact matching, coverage held separate
That move is this review’s affirmative contribution, and stating what it delivers is the one PRIMARY-hosted claim of this chapter. Cholesky Calibration Correction (CCC) fills the identified gap: at the unit-weight boundary it matches a named marginal CR2/RVE sandwich covariance exactly in finite samples for the fixed-effect block, while leaving coverage as a separately demonstrated, asymptotic operating characteristic (CL-P3-11, POSITIONING, PRIMARY HOST; G-06, G-09, G-16). The name is this review’s own coinage for the meta-analytic operationalization of the calibrated-Bayes principle, implementing the Williams and Savitsky (2021) scale-and-shape Cholesky projection mechanism. The mechanism is attributed to Williams and Savitsky; the name and the meta-analytic instantiation belong to this review (G-06).
The matching half of that statement is an exact, finite-sample, algebraic identity and the contribution, not a limitation to be hedged. At the unit-weight boundary, a posterior affinely recolored by the marginal RVE sandwich attains an exact finite-sample identity between the posterior’s marginal covariance and the named CR2/RVE sandwich target over the matched equicorrelation family \(V_j = a_j C_j(\rho)\). That this exactness is reached at the unit-weight boundary is this review’s own theoretical result and its contribution, not a restriction imposed for tractability; nonuniform weights and arbitrary heteroscedastic within-study covariance are scoped-out future extensions, named as preconditions (G-16). The object matched is named each time it appears — the marginal CR2/RVE sandwich covariance for the fixed-effect block — so the affirmative claim cannot be read as matching some other sandwich or some other posterior. Here, CR2/RVE names the practical CCC target: a companion manuscript supplies the raw likelihood-only marginal Godambe/RVE (CR0) theorem target, while CR2 is the small-sample sandwich refinement used for the fixed-effect CCC target in implementation. This identity is established by three CCC results, cited motivationally and never re-proved here: the conditional clusterwise identity and the strictly-more-general marginal fixed-effect sandwich identity of a companion manuscript — the marginal result resting on equality of matched marginal Gaussian densities with no compound-symmetric requirement — together with the covariance-matching algebra that recolors the posterior to the target and the neutrality of the correction under correct specification, both in a companion technical report. The exactness is original to this review, distinct in kind from the asymptotic Williams and Savitsky mechanism that motivates it.
Coverage is a different claim, and the discipline of this chapter is to keep it in its own sentences. Covariance-matching is an exact, finite-sample, algebraic property of the calibrated posterior’s covariance relative to that named sandwich target. Coverage is a separate operating characteristic — asymptotic in \(J\), the number of studies, and demonstrated only by simulation under specified data-generating processes, where the calibrated intervals are near nominal. The two are distinct. Exact algebraic matching does not by itself establish nominal interval coverage, and “matches exactly” is never permitted to slide into “ensures coverage” (G-09). The simulation evidence is proof-of-concept under the conditions studied, not universal, and no specific coverage percentage is cited — only that the intervals are near nominal. What the matching gives is an algebraic identity; what the simulation gives, separately, is an operating characteristic shown to be near nominal. With both held apart, the two unoccupied conjunctions of §6.2 close affirmatively: the target the frequentist lineage fixed (P1), left unoccupied by the Bayesian literature (P2, anchor CL-P2-02) and un-aimed-at under the calibrated-Bayes principle (P3, CL-P3-09), is now matched exactly in finite samples at the unit-weight boundary by a Bayesian construction (P4, anchor CL-P4-06). The per-coefficient diagnostic is deferred to a forthcoming companion paper, with coverage held throughout as its own, separate, simulation-shown characteristic.

title: 6  The gap, the contribution & claim-type discipline
section: 6.4 6.4 The claim-type discipline applied: owned vs borrowed vs deferred
text:
6.4 6.4 The claim-type discipline applied: owned vs borrowed vs deferred
The cleanest way to read the contribution is to apply the key of §6.1 exhaustively, sorting every load-bearing element into three columns (borrowed, owned, deferred) so no part of the join lacks an owner. What is borrowed is prior art, credited to its sources. The calibrated-Bayes principle (Bayesian models judged against frequentist operating characteristics; preserve the center, realign the covariance) is established in Little (2012) and Rubin (1984). The program claims only its first application to the meta-analytic RVE covariance target, never the principle itself (G-24). The Cholesky scale-and-shape projection mechanism is Williams and Savitsky’s (2021), proven asymptotically as survey-side design theory; this review borrows the mechanism, not a finite-sample guarantee (G-08). What is owned is the assembly the borrowed pieces do not constitute: the exact finite-sample identity at the unit-weight boundary over the matched equicorrelation family (distinct in kind from the asymptotic Williams and Savitsky mechanism it implements), together with the meta-analytic application of the calibrated-Bayes principle to the marginal RVE/CHE sandwich target and the CCC name itself. What is deferred is named as future territory, not claimed here: the per-coefficient design-effect-ratio diagnostic is established at survey scope by Lee (2026b); its meta-analytic, per-coefficient extension for dependent effects (the contrast-level MCMC-versus-CCC workflow for selective correction) is the scope of a forthcoming companion paper, motivated here and not pre-empted (G-22). The boundaries that fall out of this sorting (the unit-weight boundary, the matched equicorrelation family, the asymptotic-and-simulation-shown status of coverage, the deferral of the per-coefficient diagnostic) are not concessions extracted from the contribution but the exact coordinates of it.
The contribution is bounded precisely; the boundaries are preconditions, not apologies.

title: 7  Boundaries & open questions
text:
7.1 7.1 Scope of the exact identity
A precisely bounded contribution is a stronger contribution than a vaguely large one, because its claims are exactly the claims its evidence supports. A reviewer who has followed the funnel — the frequentist robust-variance-estimation (RVE) lineage owns the marginal RVE/CHE sandwich covariance as the target (Chapter 2), the Bayesian literature left it unoccupied as a named posterior calibration object (Chapter 3), the calibrated-Bayes principle of Little and Rubin licensed its import (Chapter 4), and Cholesky Calibration Correction (CCC) delivers the join (Chapter 5) — is owed an exact statement of where each result holds. This chapter supplies it. It restates three boundaries already attached to this review’s claims upstream (the scope of the exact identity in §7.1, the conditionality of the matching results and their separation from coverage in §7.2, and the survey-scope origin of the per-coefficient diagnostic in §7.3) and reads each off as a precondition: the exact coordinate at which the result is true, not a fence around what it cannot do. Each boundary doubles as an open question this review has chosen not to pre-resolve, and the funnel then closes affirmatively.
Scope echo of CL-P3-06 and CL-P4-07/08 — no new CL-ID; the scope clauses are hosted at full budget in Chapters 4 and 5.
The exact finite-sample identity holds at the unit-weight boundary over the matched equicorrelation family \(V_j = a_j C_j(\rho)\). This is the configuration, with both the unit-level likelihood weight and the random-effect (group) prior weight set to one, at which a posterior affinely recolored by the marginal RVE sandwich attains an exact, finite-sample equality between the calibrated posterior’s covariance and the named CR2/RVE sandwich target. That boundary is an exact theoretical result and an original contribution, not a restriction imposed for tractability (established in a companion manuscript, Theorems 1–2 and Corollary 1, for the unit-weight bridge, the marginal fixed-effect sandwich target, and the center-preserving recoloring; and a companion technical report, Theorem 2, for the exact covariance-matching mechanics, cited motivationally). The exactness is distinct in kind from the asymptotic Williams and Savitsky survey mechanism that motivates it. That mechanism is large-sample; the unit-weight identity is an original finite-sample result.
The scope of that exactness is the matched equicorrelation family, with arbitrary per-cluster scale \(a_j > 0\), not arbitrary SPD within-study covariance. The conditional bridge (companion manuscript, Theorem 1) is proven over the compound-symmetric class \(V_j = a_j C_j(\rho)\); nonuniform weights and arbitrary heteroscedastic or SPD within-study covariance are scoped-out future extensions, named openly, not hidden constraints (G-17). Naming this scope precisely keeps the result from inviting the “real sampling variances are heteroscedastic” objection: the equicorrelation family is the reporting scope at which the stated theorem holds, and the fixed-\(\rho\) specification is a working-model choice, not the logical site where exactness arises.
The marginal result is strictly more general than the conditional one, and the two scopes are not flattened (G-18). The companion manuscript’s marginal fixed-effect sandwich identity (Theorem 2) establishes that, under unit weights and the matched translation, the meta-analytic and survey interpretations share the same marginal fixed-effect sandwich target after the random intercepts \(\theta_j\) are integrated out. Because it rests only on equality of matched marginal Gaussian densities with no compound-symmetric requirement, the marginal identity dominates the conditional/equicorrelation bridge in generality rather than sharing its scope. The asymmetry is the precise statement: the equicorrelation scope governs the conditional bridge, while the marginal identity reaches any matched marginal Gaussian kernel. Read together, these are not two limitations but two exact results at two clearly marked scopes: the coordinates of the contribution, stated as preconditions.

title: 7  Boundaries & open questions
section: 7.2 7.2 Conditional on \(\omega^2\); covariance-matching is not coverage; the multivariate question open
text:
7.2 7.2 Conditional on \(\omega^2\); covariance-matching is not coverage; the multivariate question open
Scope echo of CL-P4-09/10 — no new CL-ID; the scope clauses are hosted at full budget in Chapter 5.
The matching results are conditional on the plug-in \(\omega^2\) and the within-study correlation. The identity-under-correct-specification result makes the conditionality concrete: when the MCMC posterior covariance already equals the target covariance, the correction is the identity transformation, neutral when the working model is right (a companion technical report, Theorem 3). The reach of that neutrality is bounded by its condition. If \(\omega^2\) is badly misspecified, the target itself is wrong, and this review claims no data-driven self-correction for a misspecified \(\omega^2\) (G-12). This is the honest precondition of any plug-in calibration: the correction realigns the posterior toward a named target; it does not certify the target was correctly specified. Naming it keeps the contribution exact rather than overstated.
The most consequential separation in this book is between two different kinds of claim, and the boundary holds them apart in separate sentences (G-09). Covariance-matching, as established by the three CCC results, is an exact finite-sample algebraic property of the calibrated posterior’s covariance relative to the named CR2/RVE sandwich target. Coverage is a separate operating characteristic: it is asymptotic in \(J\) and demonstrated only by simulation under specified data-generating processes, where the calibrated intervals are near nominal. The two claims are distinct. Exact algebraic matching does not by itself imply nominal interval coverage, and the one is never read into the other. The simulation evidence is proof-of-concept under the conditions studied, intercept-only, not universal (G-13); no specific coverage percentage is asserted, only the qualitative “near nominal.” The boundary frames a research agenda: multivariate coefficient-level operating characteristics (how the calibrated intervals behave for a full vector of meta-regression coefficients) are an open question this review does not pre-resolve. The exact matching is established at the stated scope today; the operating characteristics beyond the intercept-only regime are the next thing to demonstrate, named as open rather than quietly assumed.

title: 7  Boundaries & open questions
section: 7.3 7.3 The per-coefficient DER: motivated here, developed in a forthcoming companion paper
text:
7.3 7.3 The per-coefficient DER: motivated here, developed in a forthcoming companion paper
Scope echo of CL-P4-04/05 — no new CL-ID; the scope clauses are hosted at full budget in Chapter 5.
The final boundary is a hand-off, and it points forward to a developed contribution rather than to an absence. The per-coefficient design-effect-ratio (DER) diagnostic — which identifies which coefficients require variance correction and thereby enables selective, predictor-specific correction rather than the all-or-nothing application the frequentist sandwich provides — is established only at survey scope by Lee (2026, p. arXiv:2603.07791), who contrasts the MCMC posterior variance against a design-corrected sandwich target and performs no meta-analysis. The contrast is a design-class difference, framed neutrally, not a deficiency of the frequentist standard, and it is motivated here, not delivered here (G-22). The diagnostic is not absent from the present program: current bayesRVE outputs already include scoped DER-adjacent quantities (DER_mu, the \(\theta\) summaries, the CCC scale factors r_k, and the CHE-diagonal DER_emp). These existing outputs are narrower than the full workflow, not a placeholder for it: the complete contrast-level/default reporting workflow for meta-analytic dependent effects (the MCMC-versus-CCC comparison for pre-specified contrasts with named variance targets that would operationalize selective correction across a coefficient vector) is the scope of a forthcoming companion paper. This review motivates the diagnostic from its Bayesian survey-scope origin and points to that work; it does not pre-empt it.
With these three coordinates fixed, the funnel closes affirmatively. The boundaries are not concessions subtracted from the contribution; they are its exact coordinates. The frequentist RVE lineage fixed the marginal RVE/CHE sandwich covariance as the target (Pillar 1); the Bayesian literature left it unoccupied as a named posterior calibration object (Pillar 2); the calibrated-Bayes principle of Little and Rubin supplied the license to import it (Pillar 3); and CCC delivers the join, implementing the Williams and Savitsky Cholesky mechanism under that license to match the named CR2/RVE sandwich covariance exactly in finite samples at the unit-weight boundary over the matched equicorrelation family (Pillar 4). Coverage remains throughout a separate operating characteristic, asymptotic in \(J\) and simulation-shown, never inferred from the matching identity; and the full contrast-level per-coefficient diagnostic workflow is motivated here and developed in a forthcoming companion paper. The contribution stands exactly where its boundaries place it.
