title: Appendix A — Appendix — Claim index (CL-IDs)
text:
A.1 P0 — Foundational / inferential mismatch
Every load-bearing sentence in this book carries an inline claim-type tag — e.g. (CL-P1-05, CONDITIONAL; G-04, G-14). The CL-Pn-NN part links here. This index lists the 37 catalogued claims (CL-ID), each with its claim-type (THEOREM / CONDITIONAL / EMPIRICAL / HEURISTIC / GAP-ASSERTION / POSITIONING), the chapter that hosts it, the guardrails that bind it (G-NN, defined in the guardrail callouts at the start of each chapter), and its statement. The chapter prose is the live realization of each row.

title: Appendix A — Appendix — Claim index (CL-IDs)
text:
Claim-ID
Statement




CL-P0-01
The inferential mismatch the review addresses requires bridging three distinct literatures — robust cluster-sandwich estimation (P1), Bayesian hierarchical/multilevel meta-analysis (P2), and the calibrated-Bayes philosophy of preserving the inference center while realigning covariance (P3) — each necessary but individually insufficient to define a Bayesian RVE. (Type - HEURISTIC; Chapter - Ch1; Guardrails - G-24)


CL-P0-02
The frequentist RVE lineage made the robust sandwich the structural standard for dependent-effect meta-analysis (Hedges et al., 2010; Pustejovsky & Chen, 2024), and calibrated-Bayes theory (Little, 2012; Rubin, 1984) supplies the intellectual license to import the RVE/CHE sandwich covariance target into Bayesian inference — yet we found no published Bayesian meta-analysis method that joins those streams in the G-27 sense. (Type - POSITIONING; Chapter - Ch1; Guardrails - G-23, G-24, G-27)

title: Appendix A — Appendix — Claim index (CL-IDs)
section: A.2 P1 — The frequentist RVE lineage owns it
text:
A.2 P1 — The frequentist RVE lineage owns it








Claim-ID
Statement




CL-P1-01
Robust variance estimation (RVE) is the established frequentist field-default for meta-analysis with dependent effect sizes, built from the cluster-sandwich and refined across the lineage Hedges et al. (2010) → Tipton (2015) → Tipton & Pustejovsky (2015) → (Pustejovsky & Tipton, 2018, 2022) → Pustejovsky & Chen (2024). (Type - POSITIONING; Chapter - Ch2; Guardrails - —)


CL-P1-02
The RVE sandwich targets the marginal covariance of the effect-size vector via a working model (aggregated or correlated-effects) that may misspecify the within-study correlation, and is asymptotically consistent in m (the number of studies) without requiring the true within-study correlation to be known. (Type - CONDITIONAL; Chapter - Ch2; Guardrails - G-03, G-15)


CL-P1-03
The sandwich is consistent under arbitrary misspecification of the within-study correlation structure (which is almost never known), provided the working model’s marginal moments are calibrated to the data-generating process — the misspecification-robustness that motivates covariance recalibration. (Type - CONDITIONAL; Chapter - Ch2; Guardrails - G-25)


CL-P1-04
Small-m RVE inflates Type I error (especially m < 8) unless a named small-sample correction is applied — HTZ, AHZ, CWB, or CR2 (Bell–McCaffrey); RVE without a named correction does not control Type I error in small-m meta-analysis. (Type - EMPIRICAL; Chapter - Ch2; Guardrails - G-15)


CL-P1-05
CR2 (Bell–McCaffrey / Imbens & Kolesár (2016)) improves coverage on balanced designs but degrades under severe imbalance or skewed regressors, and its compound-symmetric / constant-subgroup-size conditions are not always met; CR2 is a design-specific tuning and an external comparator, not a universal fix or the calibration target. (Type - CONDITIONAL; Chapter - Ch2; Guardrails - G-04, G-14)


CL-P1-06
Dependent effects are handled by either the aggregated (one-row-per-study) or correlated-effects (observation-level) working model; when moderators vary within study the aggregated representation ceases to exist, whereas the correlated-effects model remains well-defined with the sandwich applied at the observation level. (Type - CONDITIONAL; Chapter - Ch2; Guardrails - G-05)


CL-P1-07
The Satterthwaite degrees-of-freedom approximation is routinely applied in frequentist RVE meta-regression to set effective df for tests/CIs; it is asymptotically justified but biased under severe imbalance or small m. (Type - CONDITIONAL; Chapter - Ch2; Guardrails - G-15)


CL-P1-08
clubSandwich (Pustejovsky & Tipton) and robumeta (Fisher & Tipton) are the canonical software implementations of frequentist RVE with small-sample corrections (CR2, HTZ/AHZ, Satterthwaite df) and are the field-standard tooling for dependent-effect meta-analysis. (Type - POSITIONING; Chapter - Ch2; Guardrails - —)

title: Appendix A — Appendix — Claim index (CL-IDs)
section: A.3 P2 — Bayesian meta-analysis & the missing analogue
text:
A.3 P2 — Bayesian meta-analysis & the missing analogue








Claim-ID
Statement




CL-P2-01
Bayesian meta-analysis is modeling-centric and genuinely flexible — weakly informative priors, hierarchical random-effects, multivariate/correlated-effects, three-level, network, and location-scale heterogeneity models — and that prior/structural flexibility is real and well-documented. (Type - EMPIRICAL; Chapter - Ch3; Guardrails - —)


CL-P2-02
We found no published Bayesian meta-analysis method that targets the RVE/CHE sandwich covariance as an explicit calibration target (in the frequentist-RVE sense), realigned under a Little/Rubin calibrated-Bayes license. (Type - GAP-ASSERTION; Chapter - Ch3 / Ch6; Guardrails - G-23, G-27)


CL-P2-03
Because the Bayesian analyst keeps the posterior as the primary inferential object, the frequentist sandwich — though theoretically motivated — does not by itself supply a named posterior target a Bayesian workflow can adopt; the calibration object (a posterior with a specific sandwich-calibrated covariance) is not a standard object in the Bayesian meta-analysis literature. (Type - POSITIONING; Chapter - Ch3; Guardrails - G-23)


CL-P2-04
Cheung’s SEM–inverse-variance equivalence requires the Level-1 error variance be fixed equal to one and does not hold under heteroscedasticity; SEM-based meta-analysis (Cheung, 2008, 2013) is therefore not a universal equivalent to frequentist RVE. (Type - CONDITIONAL; Chapter - Ch3; Guardrails - G-26)


CL-P2-05
Multivariate/multilevel Bayesian meta-analysis (Fernández-Castilla et al., 2021; Jackson et al., 2011; Van Den Noortgate et al., 2015) models within- and between-study dependence hierarchically and preserves observation-level design matrices when moderators vary within study, yet none pairs that structure with the calibrated-Bayes realignment of posterior covariance to a frequentist-RVE sandwich target. (Type - CONDITIONAL; Chapter - Ch3; Guardrails - G-23)


CL-P2-06
Recent (2023–2025) Bayesian network meta-analysis integrating composite-likelihood / calibrated-Bayesian ideas (e.g., Wang et al., 2025) is an active methodological frontier, but these entries do not target the RVE/CHE sandwich covariance as an explicit calibration target under a Little/Rubin calibrated-Bayes license. (Type - CONDITIONAL; Chapter - Ch3; Guardrails - G-23, G-27)

title: Appendix A — Appendix — Claim index (CL-IDs)
section: A.4 P3 — The calibrated-Bayes lens
text:
A.4 P3 — The calibrated-Bayes lens








Claim-ID
Statement




CL-P3-01
The calibrated-Bayes principle — Bayesian models judged against frequentist operating characteristics, “preserve the center, realign the covariance” — is established prior art in Little (2012) and Rubin (1984). (Type - CONDITIONAL; Chapter - Ch4; Guardrails - G-24)


CL-P3-02
Under the calibrated-Bayes license, an affine recoloring preserves the posterior mean while realigning the marginal covariance toward a frequentist target. (Type - HEURISTIC; Chapter - Ch4; Guardrails - G-21, G-24)


CL-P3-03
Williams & Savitsky (2021) / Savitsky & Williams (2022) established the survey-estimation↔︎Bayesian-paradigm bridge via a Cholesky scale-and-shape projection that double-weights both the unit-level likelihood and the random-effect (group) prior; they never mention meta-analysis, and their bridge is approximate/asymptotic. (Type - CONDITIONAL; Chapter - Ch4; Guardrails - G-01, G-02, G-07, G-08)


CL-P3-04
The Godambe sandwich / estimating-equation substrate (Binder, 1983; Varin, 2008; Varin et al., 2011) supplies the mathematical justification for treating covariance realignment as principled rather than ad hoc under model misspecification. (Type - HEURISTIC; Chapter - Ch4; Guardrails - G-25)


CL-P3-05
Composite likelihood (Varin, 2008; Varin et al., 2011) is asymptotic and assumes estimable low-dimensional margins; under unknown within-study correlations its operating guarantees degrade — motivating, but not supplying, a covariance correction for dependent meta-analytic effects. (Type - CONDITIONAL; Chapter - Ch4; Guardrails - G-25)


CL-P3-06
The unit-weight boundary (w_ij = 1 and w_θ = 1) is the configuration at which a posterior affinely recolored by the marginal RVE sandwich achieves an exact finite-sample identity between the posterior’s marginal covariance and the RVE target — this review’s own result, not S&W’s asymptotic mechanism. (Type - THEOREM; Chapter - Ch4; Guardrails - G-08, G-16, G-17)


CL-P3-07
CCC (Cholesky Calibration Correction) is this review’s own coinage for the meta-analytic operationalization of the calibrated-Bayes principle, implementing the Williams & Savitsky scale-and-shape (Cholesky) projection mechanism. (Type - POSITIONING; Chapter - Ch4; Guardrails - G-06)


CL-P3-08
CCC calibration is blockwise by design (β-block Cholesky on the marginal sandwich target; θ-block diagonal CE rescaling); the full-joint (β, θ) sandwich is structurally rank-deficient (rank ≤ J < K + J, incidental-parameter geometry), so full-joint Cholesky calibration is not attempted — a principled design choice, not a failure. (Type - CONDITIONAL; Chapter - Ch4 / Ch5; Guardrails - G-10, G-11)


CL-P3-09
We found no prior Bayesian meta-analysis paper that has applied the calibrated-Bayes principle (“preserve center, realign covariance”) to the RVE/CHE sandwich covariance as an explicit calibration target; this bridge from calibrated-Bayes philosophy to dependent-effect meta-analysis is this review’s contribution. (Type - POSITIONING; Chapter - Ch4 / Ch6; Guardrails - G-01, G-23, G-24, G-27)


CL-P3-10
In the CCC working model, ρ is the conditional residual (Level-1) working correlation of y_j given (β, θ_j) — not the marginal intra-class correlation, which is inflated by τ² through Σ_j(τ²) = V_j + τ²·11ᵀ; the fixed-ρ / equicorrelation specification is a working-model / reporting-scope choice, not the logical site where exactness arises. (Type - CONDITIONAL; Chapter - Ch4 / Ch5; Guardrails - G-19, G-20)


CL-P3-11
CCC (Cholesky Calibration Correction) fills the identified gap: it matches a named CR2 / RVE sandwich covariance exactly (finite-sample, at the unit-weight boundary) while leaving coverage as a separately demonstrated, asymptotic operating characteristic — the affirmative contribution statement that closes the thesis spine. (Type - POSITIONING; Chapter - Ch6; Guardrails - G-06, G-09, G-16)

title: Appendix A — Appendix — Claim index (CL-IDs)
section: A.5 P4 — Why Bayesian RVE specifically
text:
A.5 P4 — Why Bayesian RVE specifically








Claim-ID
Statement




CL-P4-01
A specifically Bayesian RVE delivers three things frequentist RVE cannot easily provide: flexible prior modeling of heterogeneity, preservation of observation-level multilevel structure, and predictor-specific (per-coefficient) diagnostics. (Type - POSITIONING; Chapter - Ch5; Guardrails - —)


CL-P4-02
Flexible Bayesian prior modeling of heterogeneity is the natural launchpad for Bayesian network-meta-analysis extensions. (Type - CONDITIONAL; Chapter - Ch5; Guardrails - —)


CL-P4-03
The unaggregated (observation-level) Bayesian likelihood preserves within-study design matrices even when moderators vary within study — the regime where the frequentist aggregated representation ceases to exist. (Type - CONDITIONAL; Chapter - Ch5; Guardrails - G-05)


CL-P4-04
A per-coefficient design-effect-ratio (DER) diagnostic reveals which coefficients need correction, enabling selective predictor-specific variance correction — a capability frequentist RVE (all-or-nothing) does not easily provide. (Type - POSITIONING; Chapter - Ch5; Guardrails - G-22)


CL-P4-05
The MCMC-vs-CCC design-effect-ratio comparison is a data-driven diagnostic for identifying correction-sensitive parameters in meta-analytic dependent-effect inference; this review motivates it and points to a forthcoming companion paper, which develops it. (Type - POSITIONING; Chapter - Ch5; Guardrails - G-22)


CL-P4-06
We found no published method that combines MCMC-based Bayesian inference with per-coefficient design-effect-ratio diagnostics for meta-analytic dependent effect sizes. (Type - GAP-ASSERTION; Chapter - Ch5 / Ch6; Guardrails - G-22, G-23)


CL-P4-07
Theorem 1 (Conditional Identity):** given a correctly specified within-study covariance and unit weights, the Cholesky-affine calibrated posterior’s covariance matches the target RVE sandwich covariance exactly in finite samples, over the matched equicorrelation family V_j = a_j C_j(ρ) (compound-symmetric class). (Type - THEOREM; Chapter - Ch5; Guardrails - G-16, G-17)


CL-P4-08
Theorem 2 (Marginal Identity):** the calibrated posterior’s marginal covariance (integrating out the random intercepts θ_j) matches the known marginal RVE sandwich target exactly under unit weights, for any matched marginal Gaussian kernel — strictly more general than Theorem 1. (Type - THEOREM; Chapter - Ch5; Guardrails - G-18)


CL-P4-09
Corollary 1 (Identity Under Correct Specification):** under correct model specification with unit weights, the calibrated and uncalibrated posterior covariances coincide — the correction is neutral when the working model is right. (Type - THEOREM; Chapter - Ch5; Guardrails - G-12)


CL-P4-10
Covariance-matching (the three CCC theorems) is an exact finite-sample algebraic property; coverage is a separate operating-characteristic claim, asymptotic in J and demonstrated only by simulation. (Type - EMPIRICAL; Chapter - Ch5; Guardrails - G-09, G-13)

section: B.6 How this review was built (reading map)
text:
B.6 How this review was built (reading map)
The review was assembled through a disciplined, documented methodology:

Framing. The positioning thesis (the four pillars P1–P4), the 37-claim framework (CL-Pn-NN, the join hub), and the 27 overclaim guardrails (G-01…G-27) were established before discovery began, defining the evidentiary scope and discipline.
Discovery. The existing literature was indexed and the search extended across the four pillars, with the two gap claims adversarially audited; a literature database and a pillar × theme map were built (this appendix’s ancestors).
Corpus. The reused corpus was staged, the acquired gap papers added, and the bibliography finalized to a 138-paper registry.
Reading. All 138 papers were read into structured critique notes, each tagged to the claim-IDs it serves, with a coverage and caveat-conflict review.
Synthesis. The claim-to-evidence map, the four pillar briefs (P1–P4), the consolidated gap-and-contribution narrative, and the annotated outline were built and externally reviewed.
Authoring. The synthesis was written into this seven-chapter book, every load-bearing sentence anchored to a catalogued claim and an exact bibliographic citation, held to the guardrails and the locked gap wordings.

Every @citekey in the chapters resolves to a bibliography entry with a DOI (citation integrity verified); the two GAP-ASSERTIONs (CL-P2-02, CL-P4-06) rest on documented systematic absence across this catalogued corpus, not on any single row of the table above.
